issue_title: Wrong table/field names used in query construction
issue_body:
While working on pull request #408, I've noticed that resolving the backend name of fields and tables is either brittle (lots and lots of duplicate code building the name from multiple object attributes) or already broken (`Table.sqlsafe` returns the correct backend name only when `Table._rname` is set or the table is not aliased, otherwise it'll return the alias which may either not exist in the database or refer to a completely different table).

I've introduced a new property `Table.real_name` in PR #408 that always returns the correct backend name of the table. However, fixing all instances of this problem is beyond the scope of that pull request so I'm reporting this here for later. `Field` doesn't have any property returning the correct full backend name/internal colname either because the `Field.sqlsafe` property relies on broken `Table.sqlsafe`. Whether to fix the existing properties or introduce new ones is open for debate.

```diff
diff --git a/pydal/base.py b/pydal/base.py
index 703deb6..d3dba06 100644
--- a/pydal/base.py
+++ b/pydal/base.py
@@ -852,6 +852,8 @@ class DAL(with_metaclass(MetaDAL, Serializable, BasicStorage)):
         table._actual = True
         self[tablename] = table
         # must follow above line to handle self references
+        if not tablename in self.tables:
+            self.tables.append(tablename)
         table._create_references()
         for field in table:
             if field.requires == DEFAULT:
```

Hi, 

I've come across an issue with subselects. 

My first problem was that i couldn't use the result of a `_select`  in an equals comparison operator, later i found out i can use `belongs` for that. Now i don't know if that will have performance penalties on some platforms, but i consider it for later concern. 

First and foremost, i need to combine two select statements that are related. 

Consider  the following model: 
```python 
db = DAL('sqlite:memory')
db.define_table('data',Field('effdt','datetime'),Field('v'), Field('s'))
db.data.insert(effdt=datetime.now(),v=10, s='mine')
db.data.insert(effdt=datetime.now()-timedelta(5),v=5,s='mine')
db.data.insert(effdt=datetime.now()+timedelta(5),v=15,s='mine')
db.data.insert(effdt=datetime.now(),v=10, s='other')
db.data.insert(effdt=datetime.now()-timedelta(5),v=5,s='other')
db.data.insert(effdt=datetime.now()+timedelta(5),v=15,s='other')
```

Now when i try to get the current v for both 'mine' and 'other'  i use the following: 

```python
print('- #0 ----------')
print (db.executesql("""
select effdt, v, s
from data d
where effdt == (
  select max(d_ed.effdt)
  from data d_ed
  where d_ed.effdt <= $NOW
    and d_ed.s = d.s
)
""",dict(NOW=datetime.now())))
```

I've tried to move this into DAL code, and have been struggling with it [for quite some time](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/web2py/ePaW1YEeKFg/hhL9D1iWavoJ). 
Now i came up with this: 

```python
d = db.data.with_alias('d')
d_ed = db.data.with_alias('d_ed')
subselect = db((d_ed.effdt <= datetime.now()) & (d_ed.s == d.s))._select(d_ed.effdt.max())
result = db(d.effdt.belongs(subselect.replace(', data AS d',''))).select(d.effdt, d.v, d.s)
```
which fortunately produces accurate results. 
But i find it quite ugly to patch the subselect string with data that is quite 'woven' with the structure of the query. It's not very 'dynamic'. 

I've tried alternate ways, like using an inner join on a query , but sqlite3 won't allow me to subselect with multiple columns apparently so that was one was off. 

So finally i came up with a little patch, shown below, to allow for an extra parameter to `_select`. Here's a sample for it in use: 

```python
d = db.data.with_alias('d')
d_ed = db.data.with_alias('d_ed')
subselect = db((d_ed.effdt <= datetime.now()) & (d_ed.s == d.s))._select(d_ed.effdt.max(), outer_scoped=[d])
result = db(d.effdt.belongs(subselect)).select(d.effdt, d.v, d.s)
```
As you can see I added `outer_scoped` in which all tables and aliases are placed that need not be mentioned in the `from` clause in the sql statement, because they refer to some name scoped outside of this query.  
Works like a charm. 

I don't have any experience with a pull request yet, but if you'd like i'll give it a try. 

Hoping for this addition to come through, cause it will save me a hell of a lot of hardcoded effective dated queries. 

Thanks. 
PS.: I don't want a workaround as proposed in the groups thread mentioned above. I have queries using tens of these constructs in one single sql statement. Hardcoding those disallows for later addition of web2py/pydal common fields to work without handcrafted maintenance and database independence. 

```diff

IDEA additional info:
Subsystem: com.intellij.openapi.diff.impl.patch.CharsetEP
<+>UTF-8
===================================================================
--- /pydal/adapters/base.py	(revision )
+++ /pydal/adapters/base.py	(revision )
@@ -39,7 +39,7 @@
 SELECT_ARGS = set(
     ('orderby', 'groupby', 'limitby', 'required', 'cache', 'left', 'distinct',
      'having', 'join', 'for_update', 'processor', 'cacheable',
-     'orderby_on_limitby'))
+     'orderby_on_limitby', 'outer_scoped'))
 
 
 class AdapterMeta(type):
@@ -1092,11 +1092,12 @@
         args_get = attributes.get
         tablenames = tables(query)
         tablenames_for_common_filters = tablenames
+        outer_scoped = [t._tablename for t in args_get('outer_scoped',[])]
         for field in fields:
             for tablename in tables(field):
                 if not tablename in tablenames:
                     tablenames.append(tablename)
@@ -1161,6 +1162,8 @@
                         if not t in important_tablenames ]
         else:
             excluded = tablenames
+
+        tablenames = [t for t in tablenames if t not in outer_scoped]
 
         if use_common_filters(query):
             query = self.common_filter(query,tablenames_for_common_filters)

```

- Move constants and non-Class functions out of result.py to this module.

  - TYPE_CONVERTERS
  - python_to_couch
  - _validate
  - _translate
  - type_or_none

issue_title: Constant Initializer only works on keys ending with weight
issue_body:
The Constant initializer only overrides the _init_weight method. Consequently, there is no good way to use existing initializers to initialize the values for bias terms, which always are set to zero.

Example code
```
data = mx.sym.var('data')
out = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden=3, name='out')
ex = out.simple_bind(mx.cpu(), data=(1, 3))
init = mx.initializer.Constant(1)
init(mx.initializer.InitDesc('out_bias'), ex.arg_dict['out_bias'])
print(ex.arg_dict['out_bias'].asnumpy())
```

The same is true for Uniform, and Normal, and while an argument could be made that *typically* when Uniform and Normal are used, biases are set to zero, this current design results in very unexpected behavior. If I apply a Constant, Uniform, or Normal, initializer to a variable, my expectation is that it will actually initialize the variable in that way! Only through digging into the source code is it apparent that this expectation is violated and even if it was made more apparent in the documentation, I don't think that's the right design anyway. The functions should work as you would expect from their name. If someone wants to treat bias terms uniquely, they should use a Mixed initializer to explicitly state that difference. Or, if initing bias to zero is common enough, allow that mode with a flag in the constructor of these initializers, so it's still explicit, but simpler than using Mixed.

For more complex initialization strategies, it makes sense that it's variable dependent.
if scale=**0** (it is 1 by default), the function random_normal is hanging up without error indefinitely in Jupyter Notebook. Python 3.5

It seems that mxnet supports multiple .rec files in `path_imgrec` as suggested in https://github.com/dmlc/dmlc-core/blob/d0afb8e946dda3310e6ed062a59f97726bf4c565/src/io/input_split_base.cc#L97
The separator is ';'. Am I correct? @piiswrong @tqchen 
BTW, I remember @tqchen onced pointed out that `path_imgrec` also accepts directory as input. So both ways are correct?

issue_title: MXNet operator profile aggregate counter issue
issue_body:
It looks the operator counter of the aggregated output is the 2x of real value. 

I used below gluon model (mainly copied from https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/gluon/gluon.html, contains 2 convolutions for each forward pass) and did profiling, the aggregated convolution counter is 4 instead of 2, the counter doubled for other OPs as well.

```
# import dependencies
from __future__ import print_function
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
import mxnet.ndarray as F
import mxnet.gluon as gluon
from mxnet.gluon import nn
from mxnet import autograd

class Net(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        with self.name_scope():
            # layers created in name_scope will inherit name space
            # from parent layer.
            self.conv1 = nn.Conv2D(6, kernel_size=5)
            self.pool1 = nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2,2))
            self.conv2 = nn.Conv2D(16, kernel_size=5)
            self.pool2 = nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2,2))
            self.fc1 = nn.Dense(120)
            self.fc2 = nn.Dense(84)
            self.fc3 = nn.Dense(10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool1(F.relu(self.conv1(x)))
        x = self.pool2(F.relu(self.conv2(x)))
        # 0 means copy over size from corresponding dimension.
        # -1 means infer size from the rest of dimensions.
        x = x.reshape((0, -1))
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x

net = Net()
# Initialize on CPU. Replace with `mx.gpu(0)`, or `[mx.gpu(0), mx.gpu(1)]`,
# etc to use one or more GPUs.
net.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Xavier(), ctx=mx.cpu())

mx.profiler.set_config(aggregate_stats=True)
mx.profiler.set_state('run')

data = mx.nd.random_normal(shape=(10, 1, 32, 32))  # dummy data
output = net(data)
output.wait_to_read()

print(mx.profiler.dumps())

```

issue_title: [Gluon] Gluon checkpoint incompatibility with older apis
issue_body:
As discussed in the other github issue #8390 , the models that have been checkpointed using gluon don't seem to be loadable via model api. There are at least couple of differences between the older format and gluon's checkpoint format that some of us found (are there more?):
1. Softmax layer doesn't exist in the gluon's checkpointed symbol file. (since softmax layer is associated with the loss layer)
1. The format of params file has changed. Older params file seem to have aux/arg keyword w/ every key where gluon doesn't do that.


Consequently, I'd like to know the following:
1. Is there a recommended way to convert older models that have been developed till now into gluon format?
1. Gluon models to older format?
1. By looking at the symbol/params file is it possible to figure out whether they are in gluon's format or not? The symbol file seems to have mxnet version but not this detail.

issue_title: mx.sym.argsort() cannot sort array with large tensor
issue_body:
For bugs or installation issues, please provide the following information.
The more information you provide, the more likely people will be able to help you.

## Environment info
Operating System: ubuntu 14.04
Compiler: GCC4.4.7
Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia): python
MXNet version: 0.11.0
MXNet commit hash :  568b5a2d3e701768ff6f270238e5edccc2f35ff1

## Minimum reproducible example
if you are using your own code, please provide a short script that reproduces the error.

```python
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np

value = mx.sym.Variable('data')
sorted_adj = mx.sym.argsort(value, axis = 2)

coord_data = np.random.rand(32, 2048, 2048)
coord_blob = mx.nd.array(coord_data, mx.gpu())
e = sorted_adj.bind(mx.gpu(), {'data':coord_blob})
y = e.forward()

result =  y[0].asnumpy()

vis = np.zeros(2048)
for i in range (2048):
    vis[int(result[4, 0, i])] = 1
cnt = 0
for i in range (2048):
    if vis[i] == 0:
        cnt += 1
print cnt
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. The provided code will give the `cnt!=0`, which should be 0 because the argsort should return all the integer from 0 to 2047.
2. If the `result[4, 0, i]` has been changed to `result[3, 0, i]` , the result will be correct again.

## What have you tried to solve it?

1.If the size of coord_data is (10000, 2048), and the axis in argsort changed to 1, then` result[8000, :]` will cover from 0 to 2047 as desired; but `vis[int(result[9000, i])]` will be incorrect again. (I guess it may because 8000<4*2048<9000)
2. By several tests, it seems that the argsort function can only deal with the first 2^24 element.

In a few zipkin traces, there were instances where server send was being emitted after client receive. It seems that "server send" represents the time when zipkin logging is finished (for a root span). However, a function being traced would already be completed when zipkin logging starts.

I believe the "server send" should be timestamped at the same time as `logging_start` since `logging_start` occurs when a function ends, and waiting until logging is completed would skew the timings (and make the span appear longer than the amount of time it actually took). We could potentially add a `logging_end` field to where the current "server send" is being logged.

This appears to be because `self.pytest_args` is initialized to an empty list in `setup.py` and then it's sent to `shlex` for parsing, but `shlex` doesn't handle lists. Defaulting to an empty string rather than a list allows tests to run, but many fail because the environment isn't set up correctly. Here's one example out of 42 test errors:

`make test` works because it provides py.test arguments:

```
python setup.py test --pytest-args="tests django_extensions --ds=tests.testapp.settings --cov=django_extensions"
```

Any of those args that are required should be the default and user flags should be added to them (or perhaps override them).

issue_title: go-sops YAML marshalling should use 4 spaces, not 2
issue_body:
The Python parser uses 4 spaces, and the Go one uses 2. This results in unnecessary noise when diffing files modified by both tools in parallel.

To Go version should use 4 spaces.

As a newcomer to secret management (used to either an ignored file or committing into memory but in a private repo), I had trouble figuring out how to apply SOPS to my current setup (e.g.would I load into memory at boot? do I store in plaintext on disk?). After enough reasoning, I resolved that:

- Loading into memory at boot isn't great for cross-language
    - Requires per-language implementation which is bad for security since higher chance of human error
    - If someone uses an autoloader like `nodemon` which restarts server upon changes during development, then that can get costly with AWS (could use caching but then we are storing in disk)
- Plaintext on disk is fine because if attacker gains server access then they can run `sops --decrypt` and look at secrets

However, to prevent all future newcomers from running into the same hurdles, I suggest we add an examples section.

I have thought of 2 configurations that might be nice to showcase:

**Catch-all secrets file:**

- File inside of configuration folder (e.g. `config/secret.enc.yml`) which contains all secrets
- Upon plaintext decryption, store without `.enc` suffix (e.g. `config/secret.yml`)
- Ignore `config/secret.yml` via `.gitignore` or similar
- In other configuration files, load in `config/secret.yml` and use in respective configurations

Here's an example setup:

https://github.com/twolfson/twolfson.com/blob/3.79.0/config/secret.enc.json

https://github.com/twolfson/twolfson.com/blob/3.79.0/config/static.js#L14

https://github.com/twolfson/twolfson.com/blob/3.79.0/bin/decrypt-config.sh

**Secrets in each file:**

This setup will likely be more sane once #24 is resolved but each secret would be managed inline in each file. This would be practical if developers want to visually cross-check test vs production values easily.

- Store all secrets inlined in encrypted configuration files (e.g. inside `config.enc`)
- Decode all files from `config.enc` to `config`
    - Bonus points for scripting editing + syncing automatically
    - https://github.com/twolfson/twolfson.com/blob/12a84f0c66bf10f80ae18e26c8d306779ce88b47/bin/edit-config-file.sh
- Ignore decrypted files via `.gitignore` for `config`

`OIDC_OP_LOGOUT_URL_METHOD` is not mentioned anywhere in the documentation.

issue_title: Prometheus Webhook and Timestamps w/ UTC offset
issue_body:
Really cool project you have going here. I've noticed some strange behavior though with the prometheus webhook and the amqp/mailer integration. Seems that new alerts get their `createTime` attribute's milliseconds field moved to the UTC offset.

e.g. `2016-11-12T17:37:02.804-05:00` gets turned into `2016-11-12T17:37:02-05:00.804Z`

This causes an exception in `parse_alert()` when mailer.py consumes the alert from the message queue.

I think I've narrowed it down to how the iso timestamp is being formed in https://github.com/guardian/alerta/blob/master/alerta/app/alert.py#L113 (and L282). Telling `replace()` to zero out the tzinfo leaves a timestamp that `parse_alert()` doesn't complain about, but I'm not sure that's the best way to solve it. Happy to submit a PR with those changes or any other ideas you might have though. Thanks!

issue_title: Issues with command line parameters in luigi 2.0.0 and base classes
issue_body:
We have traditionally kicked off our jobs using the following:

```
pythonLuigiController.py LuigiEnsembleScore --workers 5 --BaseLuigi-model-id 166 --LuigiEnsembleScore-id-value 0
```

This used to work fine, but with the new way parameters are parsed, we're unable to set parameters for other classes (besides the Task we're running) at the command line.

Essentially, we have a BaseLuigi class that is inherited by all our tasks:

```
class BaseLuigi(luigi.Task):
    model_id = luigi.IntParameter()

    def __init__(self, s3_path="s3://somepath/", *args, **kwargs):
        luigi.Task.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)

etc...

```

Then our classes utilize it as so:

```
class LuigiSomeOtherClass(BaseLuigi):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        BaseLuigi.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)

etc...

```

Make sense?  We're trying to avoid yielding the variable throughout all our classes.  We attempted using globals to fix it, but have some issues around that as well.  Any ideas?  Any way for us to still pass parameters to the base class with the new command line setup?

issue_title: cloudwatch logs filter_log_events paginator errors "same next token was received twice"
issue_body:
I believe this is related to https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/issues/3191 and that they are the same issue.
Running `boto3==1.7.30, botocore==1.10.30`

Suspected cause:
Some calls to CloudWatch Logs filter_log_events with a `nextToken` value return that same `nextToken` in the response.

In my particular case, these always seem to happen in situations in which there are no events in the payload -- the call checked a stream which had no matching data.


I had a monitoring script checking our log groups to see expected events every few minutes with filter_log_events.
Specifically,
```python
logclient = boto3.client('logs')
paginator = logclient.paginator('filter_log_events')
for page in paginator.paginate(
        logGroupName=group,
        startTime=monitor_start_window,
        filterPattern=monitor_filter):
    """ do fancy stuff """
```

when the volume of log events increased significantly, this started failing with
`Error during pagination: The same next token was received twice`

I found that this works fine:

```python
logclient = boto3.client('logs')
kwargs = {}
while True:
    response = logclient.filter_log_events(
        logGroupName=group,
        startTime=monitor_start_window,
        filterPattern=monitor_filter,
        **kwargs)
    """ do fancy stuff """
    if 'nextToken' not in response:
        break
    kwargs['nextToken'] = response['nextToken']
```

Some detailed logging reveals that the same token is sent twice in my case.
So, I have calls of the form `filter_log_events(..., nextToken='foobar123')` which return a response with `response['nextToken']='foobar123'`.

I don't know how boto3's paginators are implemented, but I suspect that's what's tripping things up.

Allowing the same token to be sent twice fixes it for me. I don't appear to receive duplicate logs, but that might be my good fortune.
For my use case, duplicates would not be problematic, so if anyone else is seeing this and looking for a solution... YMMV if you try this too.

issue_title: It is impossible to catch a CrawlerRunningException
issue_body:
This code is used to trigger a crawler:

```python
def trigger_glue_crawler(access_key_id, access_key_secret):
    glue_client = boto3.client(
      "glue",
      aws_access_key_id=access_key_id,
      aws_secret_access_key=access_key_secret
    )
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
    print("Schema crawler started.")
```

But if the crawler is already running, this exception is thrown:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 102, in trigger_glue_crawler
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/client.py", line 314, in _api_call
    return self._make_api_call(operation_name, kwargs)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/client.py", line 612, in _make_api_call
    raise error_class(parsed_response, operation_name)
botocore.errorfactory.CrawlerRunningException: An error occurred (CrawlerRunningException) when calling the StartCrawler operation: Crawler with name example_crawler has already started
```

Trying to catch the exception looks like this:

```python
def trigger_glue_crawler(access_key_id, access_key_secret):
  try:
    glue_client = boto3.client(
      "glue",
      aws_access_key_id=access_key_id,
      aws_secret_access_key=access_key_secret
    )
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
    print("Schema crawler started.")
  except botocore.errorfactory.CrawlerRunningException:
    print("Schema crawler already running.")
```

But that exception doesn't exist:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 121, in <module>
    trigger_glue_crawler(args.access_key_id, args.access_key_secret)
  File "example.py", line 105, in trigger_glue_crawler
    except botocore.errorfactory.CrawlerRunningException:
AttributeError: module 'botocore.errorfactory' has no attribute 'CrawlerRunningException'
```

That's not super surprising - I'm guessing that the exception class is dynamically generated based on a server response.  But it doesn't exist in `boto3.exceptions` either.  This snippet:

```python
def trigger_glue_crawler(access_key_id, access_key_secret):
  try:
    glue_client = boto3.client(
      "glue",
      aws_access_key_id=access_key_id,
      aws_secret_access_key=access_key_secret
    )
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
    print("Schema crawler started.")
  except boto3.exceptions.CrawlerRunningException:
    print("Schema crawler already running.")
```

results in the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 120, in <module>
    trigger_glue_crawler(args.access_key_id, args.access_key_secret)
  File "example.py", line 104, in trigger_glue_crawler
    except boto3.exceptions.CrawlerRunningException:
AttributeError: module 'boto3.exceptions' has no attribute 'CrawlerRunningException'
```

As far as I can tell, it is impossible to specifically catch this exception.  But it's a pretty normal flow that I would like to tolerate with a different code path than other exceptions.

issue_title: when importing boto3, this problem may occur "cannot import name Config"
issue_body:
Luckily it resolved itself upon uninstalling and reinstalling boto3

pip uninstall boto3

pip install boto3


Just wanted to post this in case others have similar issues.

My project creates a kinesis client and spawns multiple sub-processes that I pass the client to.  This worked when using [boto](https://github.com/boto/boto) but does not with boto3 because clients are not pickleable.  Could this feature be added?

Here's the minimal code to reproduce the issue:
```python
import boto3
import pickle
c = boto3.client('kinesis')
pickle.dump(c, open('c','w'))
```
The error I get is: _pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <class 'botocore.client.Kinesis'>: attribute lookup Kinesis on botocore.client failed

In my actual project I do something similar to:
```python
from multiprocessing import Process, Queue
import boto3

c = boto3.client('kinesis')
q = Queue()

issue_title: Kinesis / GetShardIterator: input declaration has invalid shape for Timestamp field
issue_body:
Hi,

According to [GetShardIterator API documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kinesis/latest/APIReference//API_GetShardIterator.html#API_GetShardIterator_RequestSyntax), the Timestamp member of the request is a `number`.

Yet, in the file [botocore/data/kinesis/2013-12-02/service-2.json](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/master/botocore/data/kinesis/2013-12-02/service-2.json#L664), the `Timestamp` member of the `GetShardIteratorInput` type is declared as a `Timestamp`, and according to [parse_datetime()](botocore/utils.py#L336), this would accept multiple formats, some of them as strings.

Based on actual calls to the API, I believe the documentation is correct, and this is not a timestamp but an Epoch, an Integer. I updated [Paws](https://metacpan.org/release/Paws) (an AWS SDK for Perl generated from botocore) to use an Integer for this field ([analysis](https://github.com/pplu/aws-sdk-perl/issues/217)) and the API works after the update to a number. I tried all formats of date as strings (including the ones mentioned on `parse_datetime` above) without success.

The API currently only accepts an Integer, with an Epoch.

Thank you,

issue_title: Tests fail when MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN is set
issue_body:
```
        result = runner.invoke(
            main_group,
            ['geocoding', '--reverse'],
            input='{0},{1}'.format(lon, lat),
            env={'MapboxAccessToken': 'bogus'})
>       assert result.exit_code == 0
E       assert -1 == 0
E        +  where -1 = <Result ConnectionError(u'Connection refused: GET https://api.mapbox.com/geoco...123xyz',)>.exit_code

tests/test_geocoding.py:93: AssertionError
```
where `$MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN=123xyz` is set in my environment. If I `unset MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN` it works. 

@sgillies is this just a matter of `MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN` taking precedent over `MapboxAccessToken`?
When https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/pull/203 gets merged into `mapbox-sdk-py`, the CLI will need a corresponding update.

As described in the [Python's standard library issue number 29992](https://bugs.python.org/issue29992), the JSONDecoder parameters are not offering a full parsing capability:

> Though the `JSONDecoder` already has all the hooks internally to allow for a custom `parse_string` (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/json/decoder.py#L330), this currently is not exposed in the constructor `JSONDecoder.__init__`.
>
> It would be nice to expose it. Currently, I need to do hack it: https://gist.github.com/oberstet/fa8b8e04b8d532912bd616d9db65101a

This can be extended to `parse_array` (e.g. using `tuple` instead of `list`) or even `parse_key` which would be used on JSON object key strings (e.g. `datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp` if your keys are timestamps, quite common in databases). This last one is achievable through the `object_hook` and `object_pairs_hook` parameters but it would be way less eficient as the `dict` would already be constructed with the `str` keys and you would need to start `pop`-ing and inserting new items while it could have been done at parse time.

The [last comment](https://bugs.python.org/issue29992#msg309763) offered a patch to add this params. The C accelerated version of it has not been developed yet, as it is not taking into account the new `JSONDecoder` attributes (it wasn't taking some of the old attributes either, that's why the `py_make_scanner` function needs to be used).

issue_title: Proposal: Explicit command to create a Pipfile in the current directory
issue_body:
Pipenv does not currently have an explicit “new project” command, but automatically ensures everything is in order when you run something. While this works well in general, it may leave people in the dark if the environment configuration is slightly off, as is the case of #2185.

#2185 is obviously a user error, and #2186 would help identify the problem, but the user, unless with substantial understanding to Pipenv internals, would still not be able to understand what action should be taken next.

A similar, but non-user error situation is to create nested Pipfile projects. This is suggested in #2148, which introduces the notion of “including” sub-projects. How this inclusion mechanism should be handled, if at all, is up to further discussion, but the idea of a Pipfile sub-project in itself is currently already problematic. You wouldn’t be able to create a sub-project if you create a parent project first, because all Pipenv commands will see the parent project, and refuse to create a new one.

The only reasonable way to work around this is to create the new Pipfile manually (e.g. `touch Pipfile`).  This is however still hackish, and not at all obvious unless you are knowledgable about Pipenv’s internals. With Pipenv encouraging user to let it handle Pipfile edits entirely (with `pipenv install/uninstall [package]` commands), it would also confuse users if it breaks consistency here.

The proposal is to provide an obvious way for user to initialise a virtual environment with a Pipfile created in the current directory, similar to `npm init`. This does not change the current initialisation behaviour, but only provide an additional way to initialise not only the virtual environment, but the Pipfile explicitly, right here. There are several possible ways to implement this:

* An environment variable. This is probably not a good idea, since this needs to be obvious to be useful. Environment variabls are not obvious at all, at least to me. It would also conflict with `PIPENV_PIPFILE`.
* An option to `pipenv`, e.g. `pipenv --python=... --project=.`. This is not as obvious as a top-level subcommand, but is still reasonably visible, and can be combined with other subcommands to perform tasks. It would still conflict with `PIPENV_PIPFILE`. What would `PIPENV_PIPFILE=/path/a/Pipfile pipenv --python=... --project=/path/b` even mean?
* An extra sub-command, *a la* `npm init`. This is the most obvious solution, but given the history of reluctancy to adding new commands, I can understand this may not be the preferred way to go. I do prefer this the most, and it is the only option that does not conflict with `PIPENV_PIPFILE`. It can simply throw an error if a Pipfile already exists at the target location, and `PIPENV_PIPFILE` errors if the specifies location does not exist.

The order of hashes in both Pipfile.lock and --requirements output is not deterministic: every `pipenv update` shuffles them around which makes running an update and checking for changes pointless.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type macOS High Sierra
1. Python version: `$ python -V` 3.6.4
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version` 10.1.2

NB I’m using devpi as a PyPI mirror.

##### Expected result

The hashes should be in a stable order. Easiest would be to just sort them alphabetically.

##### Actual result
 
The order changes with every run.

##### Steps to replicate

Install a bunch of packages, run update. Watch hashes permutate.

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

pipenv "hides" the virtualenv path and name, which leads to confusion and disorientation. Knowing this helps set up PyCharm IDE or simply activating the virutalenv directly from a command line.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

- When creating the virtualenv, echo the path to the virtualenv to the console.
- Have a pipenv command that echoes the full virtualenv path.
- Have `pipenv shell` prefix the virtualenv name in the shell's command prompt the same way as if one were to activate the virtualenv directly. (I am using Windows here.)

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- In general, I have found it less confusing to work directly in the virtualenv rather than using `pipenv shell`, partly because the virtualenv prefix on the command prompt tells reassures me of my current Python environment.

**Additional context**
N/A

issue_title: Where to find environment configuration defaults
issue_body:
While getting started with `pipenv` and reading thorough the documentation, I came across [_Configuration With Environment Variables_](https://docs.pipenv.org/advanced/#configuration-with-environment-variables).

Immediately, I started wondering what the defaults were. I don't see them written anywhere, and I'm not quite sure how to find them. So while I'm initially curious if anyone could point me in the right direction, I also think it would be useful to newcomers if they were listed in the section linked above.

At the moment, pipenv is not good about changing python versions in the Pipfile, and requires manually wiping out the virtualenv (``pipenv --rm``). I believe more intuitive results would be for the virtualenv to either include the version directly in the virtualenv name or in the hash calculation. 

My main argument is this would lead to the more 'expected' behavior, where a change to the python version in your Pipfile shows immediate changes on the next `pipenv` command, instead of just a warning when you go to install the next time.

This would in some ways be a breaking change, in that it would change the virtualenv location for any existing project that defines a python_version and create a new virtualenv, however I believe the added usability would be worth the change.

##### Describe your environment

1. Linux
1. Python version: 3.6.4, 3.7.0b1
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version`

##### Expected result

Changing python_version in my Pipfile should show immediate results, allowing easier changes of python versions

##### Actual result

```
Warning: Your Pipfile requires python_version 3.7.0b1, but you are using 3.6.4 (/home/frank/.local/share/v/c/bin/python).
  $ pipenv check will surely fail.
Pipfile.lock (57377a) out of date, updating to (00c27d)…
```

And pipenv continues to install packages into the existing 3.6.4 virtualenv

##### Steps to replicate

Create a Pipfile with a python_version specified, and install. Change the python_version requirement in the Pipfile to something else, and pipenv install again

I have N consumers of M partitions in a Kafka topic. Kafka messages are updates to an in-memory data structure that is initialized from (and periodically written back out to) a 3rd party DB.

I'm trying to figure out a robust solution to the following problem:
Consumer  'A' owns some subset of the partitions. Consumer 'B' comes online, initializes, and takes ownership of one (or more) partitions from 'A' and begins processing messages. Consumer 'B' then goes down and the Consumer 'A' resumes consuming the messages.

From consumer A's perspective, there was a gap in updates for a while, but eventually they resumed. In reality, A has failed to apply a subset of the updates to it's data structure because they were being handled by 'B'

If there were a mechanism to notify Consumer 'A' that it had lost ownership of the partition, it could do the appropriate flushing/retiring of data and likewise resume cleanly when it regained ownership.

Does such a mechanism exist? Is there any reason that such a mechanism wouldn't work cleanly in practice? It seems like I could pass a function pointer to the BalancedConsumer at init() and call that function at the end of _rebalance(). Thoughts? Pitfalls? Worthwhile PR?

issue_title: Have a way to make build fail if rdkafka dependency isn't built.

I think part of my problem is a result of Pykafka not always building the rdkafka extension. I don't love the install [failing so quietly](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/1b946fa288dd5eb278f397e858e887146e048e47/setup.py#L164-L172) when the rdkafka extension fails to build. 

I would love a way to make my pip installs fail if the rdkafka extension doesn't build. Perhaps the setuptools Extras notation could be used somehow? `pip install pykafka[rdkafka]`?

Hi there,

According to this logic:
https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/1b2d030b073f276c18fd6b1a64320de58d4d7904/pykafka/cluster.py#L240

It seems that PyKafka first attempts to connect to each seed host as if it were a Kafka node. Then, if this fails, it attempts to connect using the Zookeeper protocol. This means that connecting through ZK can take much longer than connecting directly to the Kafka nodes.

Here's the producer I'm using:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import logging
import time

from pykafka import KafkaClient


def main():
    s = time.time()
    client = KafkaClient(hosts='127.0.0.1:2181')
    topic = client.topics['my_channel']

    with topic.get_sync_producer(required_acks=0,
                                 linger_ms=0,
                                 min_queued_messages=1) as producer:
        for i in range(0, 1):
            producer.produce('test message')
    print time.time() - s

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

When testing, the only difference is whether `hosts` is a set of Kafka nodes or a set of ZooKeeper nodes. In the ZooKeeper case, sending a single message requires about 6s including connection time. In the Kafka case, it takes 100 milliseconds. In my use case, I need to simply send a single message as quickly as possible.

Am I doing anything incorrectly here?

It might be nice if the `KafkaClient` had an explicit `zookeeper_hosts` argument. That way, the client wouldn't have to first try the Kafka connection.

Thanks a bunch!

[I'm only reporting this based on a conversation that I was in. Others on the team may have more. We discussed adding a tool to pykafka which would monitor the offsets that consumers are currently consuming.]

issue_body: This comes out of #206. Clearly there are use cases where, if the zookeeper connection goes away and so you can't know if you still own your partitions, you should not be consuming from them. That's probably the safest option, ie to block/fail completely, and is what's the core of this commit (which would need redoing because it depends on a lot of other commits on that branch): https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/commit/3b23fadeacfec18b91d22aaf121a349a329d02cc

It looks like that we have the tools to make old versions hidden on pypi, but not using it regularly. I think it may also affects other packages in the astropy ecosystem beyond ``astropy-helpers``, so we may need to revise the release instructions, too.

```
class AstropyRegister(SetuptoolsRegister):
    """Extends the built in 'register' command to support a ``--hidden`` option
    to make the registered version hidden on PyPI by default.

    The result of this is that when a version is registered as "hidden" it can
    still be downloaded from PyPI, but it does not show up in the list of
    actively supported versions under http://pypi.python.org/pypi/astropy, and
    is not set as the most recent version.

    Although this can always be set through the web interface it may be more
    convenient to be able to specify via the 'register' command.  Hidden may
    also be considered a safer default when running the 'register' command,
    though this command uses distutils' normal behavior if the ``--hidden``
    option is omitted.
    """
```

issue_title: RandomDistributedScalarEncoder and SparsePassThroughEncoder are missing from nupic.encoders __init__.py
issue_body:
All the encoders are in the [__init__.py](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/encoders/__init__.py#L22-L35) for nupic.encoders except for
`RandomDistributedScalarEncoder` and `SparsePassThroughEncoder` are missing from nupic.encoders __init__.py

This makes it awkward when you want to import them since you need to do:
```
from nupic.encoders import random_distributed_scalar, ScalarEncoder
rdse = random_distributed_scalar.RandomDistributedScalarEncoder()
scalar_encoder = ScalarEncoder()
```

Ideally you should only need to:

```
from nupic.encoders import RandomDistributedScalarEncoder
rdse = RandomDistributedScalarEncoder()
```

issue_title: i18n bundles still registered when the bluprint is not
issue_body:
## The issue

```Flask-Collect``` doesn't collect static folders from modules which don't have a registered blueprint.

In our case if the blueprint is not registered 
``` python
 if app.config['I18N_SET_LANGUAGE_URL'] \
           and app.config['I18N_LANGUAGES']:
            app.register_blueprint(
                blueprint, url_prefix=app.config['I18N_SET_LANGUAGE_URL'])
```
the bundle will still be registered on the ```invenio_assets.bundle``` entry point [2] and ```invenio-assets``` will try to build it causing the following error

```
IOError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/home/travis/virtualenv/python2.7.9/var/instance/static/js/invenio_i18n/angularLangSelector.js'
```
[1] https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-i18n/blob/master/invenio_i18n/ext.py#L129-L132
[2] https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-i18n/blob/master/setup.py#L91-L93

The current default configuration for character encoding frequently produce false negatives. Steps to fix this should be to:

- [ ] Make use of the `cchardet.detect` output dictionary, which includes a `confidence` key about the percentage of confidence for the produced encoding detection. (eg. `{'confidence': 0.9247635369300842, 'encoding': 'ISO-8859-9'}`.
- [ ] Bump in `invenio_previewer.config` sample buffer sizes to increase probability of a positive/confident match.

issue_title: Add support for extended an expired token via management command
issue_body:
If a token has expired, it would be useful to be able to extend it through the `create_access_token` management command, rather than just blowing up with a duplicate key database error.

Proposed solution is that if you specify a token that already exists, and an expires value, then this should overwrite the existing expiry.

e.g.

```shell
# fails if token exists and has expired
python manage.py create_access_token --token foobar
# extends expiry of token by 100 days (from today)
python manage.py create_access_token --token foobar --expires 100
```

issue_title: Add user search terms to the SearchQuery log
issue_body: Because of the arbitrary complexity of the query DSL, it can often be difficult to parse out exactly what the user is searching for - which is probably the most interesting thing to store. We should allow client code to specify what the user typed in as well as the DSL blob.

issue_title: Got error 'Settings' object has no attribute 'DJOSER'
issue_body:
AttributeError at /auth/password/reset/
'Settings' object has no attribute 'DJOSER'

![screen shot 2015-07-17 at 1 14 21 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/771592/8742509/d7ef1738-2c85-11e5-88dc-256d52ce34d3.png)

Due to some third party apps which gather settings values from environment variables (django-configurations), SECRET_KEY is not always a "str"-like object but can be stringified.

```
def jwt_encode_handler(payload):
    return jwt.encode(
        payload,
        str(api_settings.JWT_SECRET_KEY),
        str(api_settings.JWT_ALGORITHM)
    ).decode('utf-8')
```

Obviously, it's better here to use __six__ for compatibility issues.

For instance, thanks to separation layer, it is possible to do a workaround by creating our own encode/decode handlers. ;)

I'm using buildozer (0.21) to generate an iOS app for the Kivy Pong code (as an initial pipeclean exercise).  It successfully builds and deploys the app, but when I open the app on the iPad, it opens then immediately closes.  I don't see even the briefest moment of the game.

I'm building on Mavericks, with Xcode 6.1.1.  No crash files are generated.

Any ideas would be much appreciated.

issue_title: Pyjnius exception Adbuddiz class not found
issue_body:
When I try to autoclass Adbuddiz activity it crashed the APK on Android with the "JavaClass.Exception" that Adbuddiz class is not found whareas it finds PythonActivity just fine 
I included the Adbuddiz.jar in my buildozer.spec 
When I decompile the APK I can't seem to find any trace of the files that where inside of the Adbuddiz.jar which means that buildozer doesn't actually include it in the build process
So what should I do to include the jar manually?

Hi,

Seems that python-for-android commit 3534a761 is responsible for: "kivy/android/PythonService.java:108: error: multi-catch statement is not supported in -source 1.5"

Thanks, Enoch.

Using: 
SDK = HOME + '/.buildozer/android/platform/android-sdk-20'
Therefore ant and not gradle

Hallo there,

since July 2014 releas a new version of NDK (v10: http://developer.android.com/tools/sdk/ndk/index.html) and use GCC 4.9. Please add it in distribute.sh if possible.

issue_title: raise exc_info[0], exc_info[1], exc_info[2] - Syntax Error
Google requiring API Target 26 in Aug/Nov 2018
issue_body:
Just wanted to put it on the devs’ radar. We’ve been limited to 19 for the last couple years, afaik, but kivy will need to support targeting 26 for new apps starting in August, and for app updates starting in November: https://android-developers.googleblog.com/2017/12/improving-app-security-and-performance.html?m=1 

If there’s currently a straightforward way to target 21 or higher then I’ll stand corrected and be very interested in hearing how.

issue_title: Server should start and end with Spyder process
issue_body: The tornado server should be deployed when Spyder starts (Similarly when the process finishes).

When a mobly test raises a TestAbortAll signal from on_fail and setup_test fails, results does not get set on the TestAbortAll signal. This causes an AttributeError instead of the expected TestAbortAll signal, because TestRunner assumes that results is set on all TestAbortAll signals.

issue_title: Limited, fixed-length compression buffer usage with a C interface
issue_body:
Here is the use case: a continuous data stream (e.g. sensor data) needs to be compressed into chunked, fixed-length records.  Each record needs to be independent, meaning no compression state is shared or otherwise linked to neighbor records.

The current C interface for encoding, specifically BrotliCompressBuffer(), does not allow writing to a limited size buffer, i.e. the output buffer appears to be required to hold the compressed output of the entire input buffer.  It is possible that the C++ interface allows this and I cannot figure out how to write a C wrapper for it.

A bit more about my use case: I am assessing whether brotli would be usable as an encoding of data samples in an international standard for time series seismic data.  The length of encoded data segments are approximately 448 to 4032 bytes.  Any suggestions within this context would be appreciated.

issue_title: Support configurable arguments on yaml specifications
issue_body: Connexion should allow some variables inside the swagger specifications to allow, for example, setting different authentication URLs, without changing the yaml itself.

Following #292 and #308 it would be also interesting to support the [x-amples](https://github.com/earldouglas/swagger-test) swagger extension which goes beyond response-only [examples](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/examples/v2.0/yaml/api-with-examples.yaml#L16) by allowing the explicit specification of [request/response example pairs](https://github.com/earldouglas/swagger-test#direct-test-specification).

This supported extension could also be the foundation for future contract testing related features, this inter-microservices contract testing workflow could potentially look something like this:

1) The **Consumer** sends a PR to the Provider's swagger.yaml by adding to the `x-amples` section the ones they are using and whose contract is important to be kept from an API consumer perspective

2) The **Provider** accepts the PR as long as is consistent with the spec.

2.1) Once accepted the **Provider** will include the validation of those `x-amples` during unit the tests, this means the Provider will setup the necessary test data to validate that is consistently able to fulfill and respect this contract, preventing breaking changes going without notice.

2.2) When a breaking change is detected, the **Provider** should contact all the **Consumers** and request them to upgrade the `x-amples` that matches the new API and a proper transition to the new API can be negotiated without breaking them in production.

2.3) Every time the **Provider** runs the unit contract tests it _POST_ the results to a broker service (this doesn't exist yet) similar to a [pact_broker](https://github.com/bethesque/pact_broker) but the difference is that we don't host the contracts there as we've just moved them to the swagger.yml `x-amples` section. This xamples_broker (to name it somehow) will act as a central point of confirmation for **Consumers** so they can be at peace that their contracts are actually being unit tested and not left to forgetfulness. This feature would be something like [this](https://github.com/bethesque/pact_broker/issues/48#issue-159976540)

3) With this `x-amples` support in place **Consumers** can now spin up a mock server out of the empowered swagger.yml file and have mocked dependencies out of the box, which takes us back to this feature request:)

issue_title: [gtk3] Inconsistent upper/lowercase shortcuts, and Primary vs. Control
issue_body:
Default shortcuts go like

    <Control><Shift>q – lowercase q
    <Control><Shift>R – uppercase R
    <Control>minus – lowercase m
    <Control>Down – uppercase D
etc.

They are shown on the UI exactly as present in org.guake.gschema.xml, should be trivial to clean up.

I'd say the desired form is what happens when the user sets these keys graphically, but hey, stupid GTK+ then calls `<Control>` as `<Primary>` instead, I don't know if it's possible (and if, how) to change it to a user-friendly name. I mean, who the heck knows what Primary is? Why is GTK+ so silly??

Terminator shows them in more user-friendly way. E.g. what's shown as `<Primary><Shift>t` in Guake is shown as `Shift+Ctrl+T` in Terminator, after the user has pressed this as the new shortcut. Maybe take a look what Terminator does differently?

(Pretty low priority compared to the modifiers not being shown up to just a few minutes ago :-))

issue_title: define strategy around dataframe index when writing to carto
issue_body:
A couple of options:

1. Renaming index if it's not named, and including it as a named column in the output
2. Not including the index in the output if the DataFrame has the default index from 0..n
3. Include index(es) as columns in the output

@michellemho, interested in mapping out and coding the possibilities?

It looks like pandas has [some good options](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/generated/pandas.DataFrame.to_csv.html) for handling this:

> **index** : _boolean, default True_
> Write row names (index)
> **index_label** : _string or sequence, or False, default None_
> Column label for index column(s) if desired. If None is given, and header and index are True, then the index names are used. A sequence should be given if the DataFrame uses MultiIndex. If False do not print fields for index names. Use index_label=False for easier importing in R

issue_title: Adding built-in resilience into jira library
issue_body:
@ssbarnea , I was thinking... We are facing a lot of issues in the test suite, which are linked to the fact that JIRA Cloud is not always responsive, and does not finish the job internally by the time the next bit of code is run (#366, #373, #374 and counting)... Well isn't that something that people will face in their own usage of the library as well?! 

I mean, a lot of code is written in the tests to check, wait and make sure that we can go through the next bit of code. Why not instead build that in the library? Like having an `ensure_transaction` flag, which if set to `True`, would check that the action has taken effect. 

It could take this form:
```
    def add_group(self, groupname, ensure_transaction=False, max_tries=5, wait_time_before_retry=1):
```
In the above example, if `ensure_transaction` was set to `True`, then a `find()` would be triggered after the `add_group` action is sent to JIRA, and if it cannot find the group, it would try up to `max_tries` times and wait `wait_time_before_retry` seconds between each try.

We could do the same for the deletes on Resources, amongst other actions:
```
    def delete(self, params=None, ensure_transaction=False, max_tries=5, wait_time_before_retry=1):
```

What do you think ?

Hi, I would like to know if it's possible to do 2-legged oauth with this library out of the box.

issue_body:
When I trying to get an archive info from "https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing.html" using `MementoClient.get_memento_info`, it raises an exception:
```pycon
>>> from memento_client import MementoClient

>>> 
>>> m = MementoClient()
>>> 
>>> a = m.get_memento_info("https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing.html")

  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memento_client/memento_client.py", line 138, in get_memento_info
    original_uri, accept_datetime=accept_datetime)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memento_client/memento_client.py", line 249, in get_native_timegate_uri
    return follow()
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memento_client/memento_client.py", line 231, in follow
    org_response.headers.get('Location'), accept_datetime)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memento_client/memento_client.py", line 219, in get_native_timegate_uri
    accept_datetime))
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/memento_client/memento_client.py", line 543, in request_head
    allow_redirects=follow_redirects)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/requests/api.py", line 96, in head
    return request('head', url, **kwargs)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/requests/api.py", line 56, in request
    return session.request(method=method, url=url, **kwargs)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 474, in request
    prep = self.prepare_request(req)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 407, in prepare_request
    hooks=merge_hooks(request.hooks, self.hooks),
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/requests/models.py", line 302, in prepare
    self.prepare_url(url, params)
  File "/home/rafid/GitHub/coala-bears/.coala-bears-venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/requests/models.py", line 382, in prepare_url
    raise MissingSchema(error)
requests.exceptions.MissingSchema: Invalid URL '/en/stable/installing/': No schema supplied. Perhaps you meant http:///en/stable/installing/?
>>>
```

This is the result when I check the headers of "https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing.html" with curl:
```
$ curl --head "https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing.html"
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Server: Varnish
Retry-After: 0
Content-Type: 
Content-Length: 0
Accept-Ranges: bytes
Date: Wed, 12 Jul 2017 06:37:27 GMT
Via: 1.1 varnish
Connection: close
X-Served-By: cache-sin18022-SIN
X-Cache: MISS
X-Cache-Hits: 0
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31557600; includeSubDomains
location: /en/stable/installing/
```

The problem is because the value of `location` on that headers is an relative url, so `MementoClient` sends a request to that `location` which is a relative url (`/en/stable/installing/`) without processing it first to be an absolute url. (from my investigation, when I look to codebase)

From my investigation, this method needs to be fixed, since it just fetch the `Location` without processing the url first (on `memento_client.py:280`)
```.py
            def follow():
                """
                a recursive func to follow redirects.
                """
                logging.debug("Following to new URI of " +
                              org_response.headers.get("Location"))
                return self.get_native_timegate_uri(
                    org_response.headers.get('Location'), accept_datetime)
```

issue_body:

- **Spark or PySpark**:  PySpark
- **SDK Version**: 1.8.0
- **Sagemaker-Pyspark Version**: 1.1.3

- **Algorithm (e.g. KMeans)**: LDA

### Describe the problem
Running inference against an LDA model endpoint with a reasonably sized dataset (> 100 documents) fails with a `Read timed out` exception. Inference jobs of less than 100 documents succeed. We believe we are using endpoint instance types with enough memory (ml.c5.9xlarge).

issue_title: Solver policies should be global constants (like C enums)
issue_body:
### Summary of the problem
Right now manticore uses strings to represent solver policies. For example, there is `MINMAX`, etc. These are encoded in the `Concretize._ValidPolicies` list. These should really be named constants with numeric values, and they should be more uniformly used.

For example, the `solve_n` method of the `State` class has `minmax` as its policy, and it's not clear if that default policy is even possible or meaningful.
```
    def solve_n(self, expr, nsolves, policy='minmax'):
```

issue_title: generalized concatenate, transpose, and batchify operations
issue_body:
It would be nice to be able to convert a sequence of vectors to a batch of vectors, or a sequence of matrices to a batch of matrices. Right now we have concatenate and concatenate_cols, but in general, we probably want an operation like numpy's concatenate that works on sequences of tensors of arbitrary size and takes an index of concatenation.

`concatenate({a, b, c}, 0)` would be equivalent to `concatenate({a, b, c})`.

`concatenate({a, b, c}, 1)` would be equivalent to `concatenate_cols({a, b, c})`.

A generalized transpose would work similarly. `tranpose(a, {1, 0})` would be usual transposition, but you could also do things like `transpose(a, {2, 0, 1})` for higher order tensors.

We should keep the old APIs around for backward compatibility and for readability, but they might just delegate to the generalized API.

Finally, it would be nice to be able to "batchify" a tensor. `batchify(a, n)` would take the leading n dimensions of `a` and make a batch of size `(d1 * d2 * ... * dn)` with dim `(d_n+1 x d_n+2 x ... x d_D)`.

I would like to open a discussion in this topic, since I already found it twice (netcdf and mdv). It's about when writing objects, in order to reduce file size, encoding variables as int (8 or 16 bits), instead of using float. The problem is how to decide the type, I see three possibilities:
1. the user encodes the data before calling write and them we use dtype.
2. the user's adds an attribute to the variable (like "write_dtype"="int8") and optionally also add scale and offset.
3. The user gives what type he want while calling write

An utility for number 1. is already in PR #266, but this is far from optimal since it may destroy data

Number 2. is my favorite and would be just a question of defining the attribute name and implement

I am not a fan of number 3. since it would need to add an option in all write routines; however one thing plays in its favor: NetCDF just use signed ints and MDV just UNsigned ones. However I believe we could recalculate the scale and offset to solve this problem without user interference.

What do you think? do you see any problem or other alternatives?

=================================== FAILURES ===================================
____________________________ test_from_fiducial_sky ____________________________

    def test_from_fiducial_sky():
        sky = coord.SkyCoord(1.63 * u.radian, -72.4 * u.deg, frame='fk5')
        tan = models.Pix2Sky_TAN()
>       w = wcs_from_fiducial(sky, projection=tan)

/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/gwcs/tests/test_wcs.py:153:
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _
_ _ _ _
/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/gwcs/wcstools.py:62: in wcs_from_fiducial
    fiducial_transform = _sky_transform(fiducial, projection)
/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/gwcs/wcstools.py:112: in _sky_transform
    sky_rotation = models.RotateNative2Celestial(lon, lat, lon_pole)
/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/astropy/modeling/rotations.py:231: in __init__
    super(RotateNative2Celestial, self).__init__(lon, lat, lon_pole, **kwargs)
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ __ _ _ _

self = <[AttributeError("lon") raised in repr()] RotateNative2Celestialobject at 0x7f4499dd52e8>
lon = <Longitude 93.39212060632418 deg>, lat = <Latitude -72.4 deg>
lon_pole = 180, kwargs = {}, qs = [True, True, False]

    def __init__(self, lon, lat, lon_pole, **kwargs):
        qs = [isinstance(par, u.Quantity) for par in [lon, lat, lon_pole]]
        if any(qs) and not all(qs):
>           raise TypeError("All parameters should be of the same type - float or Quantity.")
E           TypeError: All parameters should be of the same type - float
```
Full test log [here](https://ci.debian.net/data/autopkgtest/unstable/amd64/g/gwcs/20170711_075125/log.gz). There is also a [Debian bug #868115](https://bugs.debian.org/868115).

It seems that `gwcs.util` does not use units in `_compute_lon_pole()`; I am however unsure about side effects when just adding `u.deg` there.

At any point in future you can request a re-review of the package if you believe any of the scores should be updated - contact the coordination committee, and we’ll do a new review. Note that we are in the process of redesigning the http://affiliated.astropy.org page to show these scores (but not the comments). Finally, please keep the title of this issue as-is (“Astropy Affiliated Package Review”) to make it easy to search for affiliated package reviews in future.

issue_title: on error, error occurs in the error processing
issue_body:
i am using the gcloud package to download files from google cloud storage, python 2.7 from a google compute engine.  i am launching multiple processes using the multiprocessing package.  there is an occasional error, but the actual error message is obscured because an error occurs getting that message.  here is the stack trace:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "mirna_isoform_matrix/download_isoform.py", line 39, in download_file
    bucket = client.get_bucket(bucket_name)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gcloud/storage/client.py", line 167, in get_bucket
    bucket.reload(client=self)

    _target_object=self)

issue_title: Mismatches of properties between Table obtained from Client.list_tables and TableReference obtained from QueryJob.destination
issue_labels: [{'id': 82126549, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20bigquery', 'name': 'api: bigquery', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604243, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p2', 'name': 'priority: p2', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'd93f0b', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Background:

I was trying to answer this [stackoverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47838558/how-to-get-saved-query-from-view-in-bigquery) whose goal is to try and obtain the query that created a particular view. As such, my line of thinking was to find the `QueryJob` object whose `destination` attribute yields a `TableReference` pointing to the view whose sql statement I am after. Once the `QueryJob` object is found, then I can use its `query` attribute to get the sql statement.

### Code:

```python
from google.cloud import bigquery

my_view_id = 'view_table_id'
my_project = 'my project'
bq_client = bigquery.Client(my_project)

for job in bq_client.list_jobs():
    if job.destination.table_id == my_view_id and job.destination.project == my_project:
        print(job.query)
        break
```

### First Issue:

The snippet above does not yield anything. This is because `job.destination` returns a `TableReference` object whose `table_id` attribute yields something completely different from the actual view's `table_id`. This got me thinking that perhaps this is because `job.destination` is just a `TableReference` and therefore does not have all the necessary properties. As a result, I decided that I should get the actual table and try making the comparison again.

### Attempted fix:

```python
from google.cloud import bigquery

my_view_id = 'view_table_id'
my_project = 'my project'
bq_client = bigquery.Client(my_project)

for job in bq_client.list_jobs():
    try:
        actual_table = bq_client.get_table(bigquery.Table(job.destination))
    except:
        continue
    if actual_table.table_id == my_view_id and actual_table.project == my_project:
        print(job.query)
        break
```

### Second Issue:

This snippet also fails to get the query for my targeted view. Even getting the `Table` object does not refresh the `table_id` attribute.

### Questions:

1. How do we go about refreshing the `table_id` attribute to make sure we can find the `QueryJob` that created our targeted view?

2. Is there a better way of getting the query without having to rely on finding the job that created the view?

Thank you,

Abdou

issue_title: dumping a lambda function coming from sympy.lambdify not working
issue_body:
I have described the issue [here]()
Here is a minimalist example:
    from pdb import set_trace
    import sympy as sp
    import dill as pickle

    x, y = sp.symbols("x, y") 

    f_example = 2*x**2 + 2*x*y
    f_lbda= sp.lambdify((x, y),f_example ) 
    pickle.settings['recurse'] = True 
    # works 
    fileW = open('file_where_I_dump.dill', 'wb')
    pickle.dump([f_lbda, f_lbda], fileW)
    fileR = open('file_where_I_dump.dill', 'rb')
    f_lbda_loaded = pickle.load(fileR)

And a more complicated one where dill is working nicely to import sympy expressions, but not the corresponding lambda functions:

    import sympy as sp
    import dill
    import pickle

    # declare multiply symbolic variable at the same time 
    x, y , Alpha, Ksi, x1x, x1y, x2x, x2y, x3x, x3y, N, nx, ny  = sp.symbols("x, y , Alpha, Ksi, x1x, x1y, x2x, x2y, x3x, x3y, N, nx, ny ") 
    # gap function and penalty stiffness 
    gN, kPenN = sp.symbols("gN, kPenN")
     
    """
    Explanations for the symbolic variable declared
    """
    S = sp.Matrix([x, y])
    x1 = sp.Matrix([x1x, x1y])
    x2 = sp.Matrix([x2x, x2y])
    x3 = sp.Matrix([x3x, x3y])
    # normal to the curve taken at Ksi = Ksi_bar
    N  = sp.Matrix([nx, ny])
    # control points
    b0 = x2 + 0.5 * (x1 - x2)
    #b3
    b3 = x2 + 0.5 * (x3 - x2)
    #b1
    b1 = b0 + (x2- b0) * Alpha
    #b2
    b2 = x2 + (b3 - x2) * ( 1 - Alpha)


    # Berstein polynomials
    # B1 
    B0 =  (1./8.) * ((1 - Ksi)**3)
    # B1
    B1 = (3./8.) * (1 - Ksi)**2 *  (1 + Ksi)
    # B3
    B2 =  (3./8.) * (1 - Ksi) * (1 + Ksi)**2
    #B4
    B3 = (1./8.) *(1 + Ksi)**3


    # Berstein polynomials first order derivative
    B0_Ksi = sp.diff(B0, Ksi)
    B1_Ksi = sp.diff(B1, Ksi)
    B2_Ksi = sp.diff(B2, Ksi)
    B3_Ksi = sp.diff(B3, Ksi)


    # expression of the position vector along the 
    # Bezier polynomial depending on the convective 
    # coordinate Ksi
    x_interp = b0 * B0 +  b1 * B1 + b2 * B2 + b3 * B3
    x_Ksi = sp.diff(x_interp, Ksi)
    #second order derivative
    x_KsiKsi = sp.diff(x_interp, Ksi, 2)

    #check the shapes
    for el in [x_Ksi, x_KsiKsi]:
        if el.shape != (2,1):
            raise Error('incorrect size')

    """
    Important: all these matrixes will be evaluated numerically
    for Ksi = Ksi_bar in the main file
    """
    # Matrices used to compute different contribution to contact terms and so on 
    Bn  = sp.flatten([N, -B0 * N, -B1 * N, -B2 * N, -B3 * N])
    Bn_Ksi  = sp.flatten([sp.zeros(2,1), B0_Ksi * N, B1_Ksi * N, B2_Ksi * N, B3_Ksi * N])
    Bt      = sp.flatten([x_Ksi, -B0_Ksi * x_Ksi, -B1_Ksi * x_Ksi, -B2_Ksi * x_Ksi, -B3_Ksi * x_Ksi]) 
    Bn = sp.Matrix(Bn)
    Bn_Ksi = sp.Matrix(Bn_Ksi)
    Bt = sp.Matrix(Bt)
    H_KsiKsi = x_Ksi.dot(x_Ksi) - gN * N.dot(x_KsiKsi)
    B_Ksi  = (H_KsiKsi**(-1)) * ( Bt + gN*Bn_Ksi )
    b_KsiKsi = x_KsiKsi.dot(N)
    #check the shapes
    for el in [Bn_Ksi, Bt, B_Ksi]:
        if el.shape != (10,1):
            raise Error('incorrect size')


    # We now have all the elements to compute the contribution of ONE contact element to the residuum
    # Note that I did not take into account the vector of variations because I do not use the 'test function'
    # in my code
    dWN = kPenN * gN * Bn_Ksi

    """
    # just to check that the 2 expressions are equal
    # except that norm() adds ABS that are not convenient to manipulate ..
    expr1 = x_Ksi.norm() ** 2
    expr2 =  x_Ksi.dot(x_Ksi)
    set_trace()
    """

    # computing the contribution to the stiffness locally. I used directly
    # the form provided in 9.239
    DdWN = kPenN * (   Bn.dot(Bn.T) - gN * ( Bn_Ksi.dot(B_Ksi.T) + B_Ksi.dot(Bn_Ksi.T) + b_KsiKsi * B_Ksi.dot(B_Ksi.T) - (gN / (x_Ksi.dot(x_Ksi)) * (Bn_Ksi + b_KsiKsi * B_Ksi).dot((Bn_Ksi + b_KsiKsi * B_Ksi).T)))) # only the terms corresponding to the variation of the dof of the slave node are
    # interesting to me in the case of contact with a rigid seg 


    # Saving the objects:
    with open('normal_contact_Bezier.dill', 'wb') as f:  # Python 3: open(..., 'wb'):
        dill.dump([dWN , DdWN ] , f)


    #to load the objects as they are
    with open('/Users/marco.magliulo/repos/COMMON_ROOT_MYPROG/Python/OOP_2DLattice/Symbolic_computations/normal_contact_Bezier.dill', 'rb') as f:  # Python 3: open(..., 'rb')
        dWN , DdWN = pickle.load(f)


    dWN_lbda= sp.lambdify((x, y , Alpha, Ksi, x1x, x1y, x2x, x2y, x3x, x3y, N, nx, ny, gN, kPenN), dWN) 
    DdWN_lbda= sp.lambdify((x, y , Alpha, Ksi, x1x, x1y, x2x, x2y, x3x, x3y, N, nx, ny, gN, kPenN), DdWN) 


    with open('normal_contact_Bezier.dill', 'wb') as f:  # Python 3: open(..., 'wb'):
        dill.dump([dWN_lbda, DdWN_lbda] , f)


    with open('/Users/marco.magliulo/repos/COMMON_ROOT_MYPROG/Python/OOP_2DLattice/Symbolic_computations/normal_contact_Bezier.dill', 'rb') as f:  # Python 3: open(..., 'rb')
        dWN_lbda, DdWN_lbda= pickle.load(f)


please do not hesitate to contact me if you require more info.

issue_title: EC2 NoDevice error with boolean values.
issue_body:
It appears that the `NoDevice` field in the `BlockDeviceMapping` was updated in #858 to be a boolean.  Unfortunately now, the generated templates are failing to deploy, regardless of what value you use for the field.  When set to either True or False, I receive the following error when trying to deploy a generated template via CloudFormation: `Value of property NoDevice must be an object`

Looking at the [docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-blockdev-mapping.html#cfn-ec2-blockdev-mapping-nodevice)  the type is listed as boolean, however the last example on the page `Unmapping an AMI-defined Device` shows the use of an empty map, `{}`, and not a string or boolean value.

I saw that @tweakster noted he received a error stating this should be a string, but I have tried multiple regions, and always receive the same error if an empty map is not used.

We have been using this value successfully in previous versions, and I propose that the change be reverted.  I will work on submitting the required code change, later today, and can provide a sample of working code using this empty mapping as well.

issue_title: Relations in zinc analysis not properly rebased/made-portable
issue_body:
We recently tracked down an incremental compile issue in `1.8.0` to two cooperating bugs:

1. Analysis fetched from other machines does not have its Relations properly rebased (in particular, the `libraryClassNames` File keys retain the absolute file paths from the producing machine), which means that [no classNames are select here](https://github.com/sbt/zinc/blob/1.1.x/internal/zinc-core/src/main/scala/sbt/internal/inc/IncrementalCommon.scala#L446).
    * Although our AnalysisMappers appear to be [correctly installed](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/analysis/PortableAnalysisMappers.scala#L17), the mapper does not appear to be [applied to Relations at write time](https://github.com/sbt/zinc/blob/1.1.x/internal/zinc-persist/src/main/scala/sbt/internal/inc/binary/converters/ProtobufWriters.scala#L588).
2. Because of `1.`, the `exists` call [on the next line](https://github.com/sbt/zinc/blob/1.1.x/internal/zinc-core/src/main/scala/sbt/internal/inc/IncrementalCommon.scala#L447) returns false (ie, indicates that no invalidation has occurred)... but should potentially trigger a warning, and should almost certainly return true.

now that we're adequately entangled in platform-specific deps that are often times resolving (or failing to resolve.. or build..) against pypi, it would probably make sense for us to go ahead and start building and releasing a binary pex of pants along side (or in place of) sdists. this should eliminate most customer issues related to resolves and/or resolve-time builds of native deps at pants install/bootstrap time and ensure a consistent and tested shared set of backing wheels for running pants in most cases.

at Twitter, we deploy pants only via pex using a custom build and release process. having a formally released OSS pex that we can directly consume will help us stay closer to shared releases vs direct sha consumption.

issue_title: Statsdb flag gives an unhelpful output if the sqlite file doesn't exist.
issue_body:
This class doesn't validate the path it is passed as a flag or as a default value.
If passed a bogus file the entire pants run errors with an Exception.

This can be repro'd by overriding pants_bootstrapdir, done by adding 
```pants_bootstrapdir: %(buildroot)s/.bad.location``` to the pants.ini.

Two options I can see to address this would be:

1) adding a check to see if the file exists with os.path.isfile 
or better yet, 
2) creating the database if it doesn't exist. 

Doing the file check alone isn't quite as simple as it might seem
because it doesn't really solve the problem. I haven't  tracked 
down where but this file is being created in the default case
so finding out where that is done would be the majority of the battle
for the robust fix.

issue_title: NailgunExecutor should more strictly check process information to safeguard against stale metadata reuse in the ProcessManager lifecycle.
issue_body:
A user reported an issue:

```
20:31:48 06:28             [zinc]
                           ==== stderr ====

                           ==== stdout ====

WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -15 3075!
20:29:54 04:34           [compile]
                       compile(src/jvm/com/foursquare/apiserver/api2/services:services) failed: failed to kill pid 3075 with signals (15, 9)WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -9 3075!

20:31:48 06:28             [zinc]
                           ==== stderr ====

                           ==== stdout ====

WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -15 3075!
20:30:03 04:43           [compile]
                       compile(src/jvm/com/foursquare/services/batch:batch) failed: failed to kill pid 3075 with signals (15, 9)WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -9 3075!
```

it was determined that nailgun management in pants runs in disparate buildroots may be occasionally colliding due to non-strict checking of process metadata in the instance of stale pids.

we currently pass a parameter to the nailgun cmdline specifying the buildroot. this is checked during an `ng-killall` (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_executor.py#L41) but not in the inline lifecycle management of NailgunExecutor (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_executor.py#L162).

the immediate remedy here appears to be to extend this checking throughout.
I ran into some trouble working with subsystems.  I wasn't able to find any documentation, we should probably add some good documentation on how subsystems are used, pitfalls, etc.

issue_title: python_binary_create task does not respect default platforms from python-setup
issue_body:
The python_binary_create task does not take default platform arguments from python-setup into account when creating a pex file; only the platforms from the binary target in question are plumbed through to pex builder.

This task needs to prefer target-level constraints, and if those are empty, use the config provided by the python-setup object.

issue_title: Engine Refactor: Silent failure for un-picklable inputs to the MultiProcessEngine
issue_body:
Passing un-picklable inputs to the MultiProcessEngine (for example, using an inner/nested class as Configuration) causes the following to hang silently:
