./pants test tests/python/pants_test/engine/exp:engine -- -k test_multiprocess_engine
```

issue_title: Add support for slurping up package.json as the basis of a NodeModule target
issue_body:
There is discussion of this in the comments on @chrispesto's [design doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1i5CeptGabqAImFZmi7v4dfRcADt6jgl7Lxhvw8CN53M/edit#heading=h.etho4skge340) but a conversation with @huckphin re-crystallized the need for this in the near to medium (hah!) term.

issue_title: Support invalidation generically via an option
issue_body:
Currently, manual invalidation is only supported via `./pants invalidated`. This is problematic for a few reasons:
1. "invalidate" is not a verb in pants user-space, it's only one we use internally in the codebase
2. The `invalidate` goal acts globally, invalidating all targets across all tasks

As @benjyw suggested [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/4660#pullrequestreview-43801196), a global recursive `--force` flag might take the place of the `invalidate` goal and also allow more targeted invalidation at the task level when placed to the right of a fully qualified goal name on the command line. To-boot, `--force` likely makes more intuitive sense in pants user-space; ie: force this command to run instead of skipping things.

issue_title: Option to prevent duplicate images from webcam when using ffmpeg Reader.get_next_data()?
issue_body:
Below is a feature proposal, but I would be happy to turn it into a pull request.

Problem
----------

As far as I can see, when used with a webcam stream, `FfmpegFormat.Reader.get_next_data()` always returns an `Image`, regardless of whether-or-not a _new_ image is actually available from the webcam. Thus, the image returned may be either new or a duplicate of the previous one. 

This is probably intentional, but, if the user is unaware and expects a kind of blocking behavior (as I was/did initially...), it may lead to unexpected results. 

For example, in the code below, without additional timing control, we will get many unnecessary calls to `update_screen` and `append_data`, with duplicate frames in the resulting video file (assuming the loop frequency is higher than the camera framerate).

    while True:
        new_image = reader.get_next_data()
        update_screen(new_image)
        writer.append_data(new_image)

To fix this, the user would need to check for duplicate images or implement some other form of timing control.

Relevance
-----------

On the one hand, I can see why such a check for duplicates may be considered an implementation detail, but, on the other hand, I guess there are very few use cases where the user will _not_ benefit from a built-in (but optional) check for duplicates.

Proposed solution
--------------------

A convenient way to implement a check for duplicates in the ffmpeg `Reader.get_next_data()` would be to provide the _option_ to return `None` in case the image is a duplicate of the previous image (e.g. the `only_new` argument below). A user can then time his/her loop using a simple truth test:
    
    new_image = reader.get_next_data(only_new=True)
    if new_image is not None:
        update_screen(new_image)
        writer.append_data(new_image)

Setting `only_new=False` by default would ensure backward compatibility.

To reduce the performance penalty resulting from the equality test, we can test a limited number of pixels, e.g. let `only_new` take a (percentage) value instead of a boolean.

Alternatives
-------------
Maybe similar functionality is already available but I haven't found it yet?

Possible alternatives would be:

- ignore this issue and just let the user deal with it
- let the `get_next_data()` method block until a new frame is available
- use an event-driven approach, e.g. a `data_available` event (not sure if this can be implemented via ffmpeg and/or subprocess)
Is there a reason GetContentString doesn't take a mimetype as a parameter?

issue_title: pin Kolibri to specific versions of installers and wrappers
issue_body:
In the same way that the Kolibri code base is tied to a specific version of other dependencies like the Perseus Renderer plugin, it should also be tied to specific versions of installers and other wrappers. This will help us achieve more deterministic build artifacts.

"installers" and "wrappers" include:

* Windows installer
* Windows GUI
* Android APK
* Platform-specific PEX variations

What ever mechanism we use for pinning installers, the outcomes should be that a particular version number of Kolibri (or commit hash) uniquely determines what the build artifacts will look like. Checking out and building Kolibri should always result in the same artifacts.


----

refs: 

* #1991
* #2202
* https://learningequality.slack.com/archives/C0LE9NLCE/p1505882504000037

issue_title: Detect multiple A records of server hostnames

Currently the client does not look at the IP when it removes a server due to a problem, and therefore takes the DNS hostname out of the active servers, which leads to no remaining active servers and an exception. issue_title: Handle HTTP success status codes beyond 200
issue_body:
There are [many HTTP status codes that indicate different kinds of successful responses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes#2xx_Success). "200 OK" is the most widely used, but many RESTful APIs use "201 Created" in response to creating resources, and "204 No Content" in response to deleting resources.

This project is hardcoded to only accept the 200 status code as success, which means that if the API ever returns a different, non-200 status code to indicate a successful response, the SDK will throw an exception. This project should work with success status codes beyond 200.

When using the `requests` library, you can use [`response.ok`](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/api/#requests.Response.ok) to determine if the response was successful. You can also use [`response.raise_for_status()`](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/api/#requests.Response.raise_for_status) to raise an exception if necessary, rather than trying to determine if the request was successful and raising an exception if it wasn't.
If a malformed WARC is passed to ipwb indexer, do not echo errors from the WARC parsing module but report the error to the user.

      client_kwargs['https_proxy'] = ('%s:%s@%s:%s' % (proxy_info.proxy_user,
                                                      proxy_info.proxy_pass,
                                                      proxy_info.proxy_host,
                                                      proxy_info.proxy_port)
                                      if proxy_info else None)
    else:
      client_kwargs['https_proxy'] = ('%s:%s' % (proxy_info.proxy_host,
                                                proxy_info.proxy_port)
                                      if proxy_info else None)
```


Thanks,
Faisal

issue_title: Slash zero (/0) after prefix length in IPv6 flowspec source and destination
issue_body:
##### Summary

IPv6 Flowspec routes have a /0 after the prefix length in the source/destination fields like 2001:beef:cafe:0:10:85:7:21/128/0. There is a vendor using a similar notation to communicate information additional to the prefix length, but in this case the data appears in the dedicated source and destination match fields causing us issues downstream of it.
See JSON example below.

##### OS
```
Linux
```

```
Linux bgtnj03vmh01afx 4.4.0-97-generic #120-Ubuntu SMP Tue Sep 19 17:28:18 UTC 2017 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

##### Version
```
ExaBGP : 3.4.26
Python : 2.7.12 (default, Nov 19 2016, 06:48:10)  [GCC 5.4.0 20160609]
Uname  : #120-Ubuntu SMP Tue Sep 19 17:28:18 UTC 2017
```

##### Installation
```
git clone
```

##### Environment 

```
blank
```

##### Configuration

```
group EXA_AFX1 {
        local-as 65060;
        peer-as 65060;
        hold-time 90;
        adj-rib-out false;
        router-id 192.168.195.132;
        family {
          ipv4 flow;
          ipv6 flow;
        }
# note that multiple instances of exabgp may bind to the same IP address
# if their respective peers are distinct
        local-address 192.168.195.132;
#
        process received-routes {
                # deliver updates on API to uafx
                run /opt/afx/uafx.py;
# use JSON encoder for receiving updates
                receive-routes;
                encoder json;
        }
        neighbor 192.168.101.120 {
                description "peer for Ph01ts";
        }
```

##### Program output
Below is the JSON received on the API
```
'{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1531860159, "host" : "bgtnj03vmh01afx", "pid" : "10908", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 3, "type": "update", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.101.123", "address": { "local": "192.168.195.132", "peer": "192.168.101.123"}, "asn": { "local": "65060", "peer": "65060"}, "message": { "update": { "attribute": { "origin": "igp", "local-preference": 100, "extended-community": [ 9225060886715039744 ] }, "announce": { "ipv6 flow": { "none": { "flow-0": { "destination": [ "2001:beef:cafe:0:10:85:7:21/128/0" ], "source": [ "2002:beef:cafe:0:10:85:10:1/128/0" ], "string": "flow destination 2001:beef:cafe:0:10:85:7:21/128/0 source 2002:beef:cafe:0:10:85:10:1/128/0" } } } } } }} }'
```

##### Steps to reproduce
```
Not intermittent, the IPv6 prefix always appears in that representation.
```

##### Importance
<!--
-->
```
Production blocker; donwstream system doesn't understand IPv6 address.
```

# Overview

Some mature packages provide own exception hierarchy (there are pros and cons). For now we have just a base `tabulator.errors.Error`. 

Also first implementation doesn't handle processors exceptions (processor has to do it by itself). There are pros and cons of it. Need to be investigated after some usage feedback with some processors written.

# Analysis

pwalsh said:

> Maybe we expose a base ProcessorError, inherit from it in our own processors, and in docs encourage that custom processors use it to catch errors. Then, tabulator itself just has to listen for ProcessorError when streaming data through processors.

# Tasks

- [ ] remove `Error`
- [ ] expose `exceptions` module as in other FD packages
- [ ] Tasks

issue_title: Install DB migrations on driver startup
issue_body:
Install DB migrations on driver startup. We can just open our own database connection like the cli script does and arrange for alembic output to get to the neutron logs.

 - [ ] Install DB migrations on driver startup
 - [ ] Add alembic to requirements.txt so that we're sure it's available on all the neutron servers
 - [ ] Plumb alembic output to the neutron logging

issue_title: Cannot use HTML tags in children text
issue_body: It seems like when I add HTML to an html_component children text, it is somehow keeping the browser from parsing it into anything but plain text. For example, trying to use an `` tag results in the actual text `` instead of a link. An example to clarify: ` html.Div('If you\'re interested in viewing trends, please' + ' check out Google Trends' + ' for more information.' ),` This results in being displayed on the webpage as: `If you're interested in viewing trends, please checkout Google Trends for more information. `

issue_title: kwarg to collect additional data from CAISO lmp
issue_body: `get_lmp` could be returning congestion price, etc in addition to LMP. Add a kwarg `all_data=False` that, if set to `True`, returns all the data types instead of just LMP.

This seems odd compared to the rest of the build output:

```
Removing intermediate container 4c64e3f3d33a
Successfully built 2ec15613169e
-----> Pushing image to private registry

-----> Launching... 
       done, www:v2 deployed to Deis
```

We should strip out that extra line between "pushing" and "launching" if there's no output.

Section http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/backing_up_data/#finishing-up doesn't really work for me because of `kwargs['created']` check here: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/controller/api/models.py#L1192 and, probably, all other methods. So `save()` method in the link above doesn't really do anything on restored database, as `kwargs['created]` is False.

```

At this point, vagrant was consuming about 4gb of memory. From the docs I expected it to use around 2gb. The memory use kept growing over the next few minutes until it was taking 5.7. This machine only has 8gb, so with Chrome and everything else running it brought my machine to a halt, swapping like crazy. I ran "vagrant halt", which took about 10 minutes to eventually force shutting down the 3 VMs.

Looks like I can deal with the "failed getting response" issue [here](https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/1989) but I'm wondering about the unbounded memory growth.
* `size` is missing from the async-transfer `--sync-level` option.

issue_title: Setup an install extra for getting `python-jose`, i.e. `globus_sdk[jwt]` or similar
issue_body:
It's kind of lame to ask people to enable optional features by explicitly installing `python-jose`.
The cool thing to do with setuptools is to declare an extra. For example, you could ask for a version + extras like so:
```shell
$ pip install 'globus_sdk[jwt]==0.7.1'
```

Setuptools [supports this](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#declaring-extras-optional-features-with-their-own-dependencies) with minimal effort. The only big question I have is how we go about testing the results.

@jaswilli, @corpulentcoffee Are there other concerns we might have about adding this as an extra? Is there a better name than `jwt` for the `python-jose` extra?

If we do this, updates to the `GlobusOptionalDependencyError` messaging should probably be applied.
It will be nice if there is documentation for installation especially if there is a pip install available.
I am not sure if this unit test is that beneficial to us anymore as we check the values of the feature calculators individually.

[2018-07-15 14:47:23,259] ERROR in main: Server Error: maximum recursion depth exceeded in comparison
127.0.0.1 - - [15/Jul/2018 14:47:23] "POST /compare HTTP/1.1" 500
Time to drop python 2 support (like has been done in the other python ethereum libraries)

### What is wrong?

`vm.previous_hashes` hits the database a lot. Caching helps, but it still involves ~`255 * n` DB reads when importing n blocks. There are probably a lot of blocks that never use the `BLOCKHASH` evm opcode, and so these reads are wasted.

### How can it be fixed

Send a callable (like `lambda: self.previous_hashes`, though probably not literally that) to execution_context that it will invoke, as needed.

I would put a [Good First Issue], but the API is changing a lot, and I don't want to ask a new contributor to do a bunch of ugly rebases.

issue_title: Need a way to limit number of connected peers
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

We accept incoming peer connections but there's no way to limit the number of simultaneous connections.

### How can it be fixed

Add a CLI flag to specify max number of peers, and have `p2p.Server` reject new connections once we reach that

issue_title: Add contract test with fixedMxN argument and return value

### What was wrong?

`fixedMxN` types are not supported. Various groups (including Casper), need them.

### How can it be fixed?

#828 only fixed a specific issue raised by a team that wants to use fixed types. It is likely that there are other things that need to be addressed before everything is resolved. The best way to know is to add a new contract in the tests that accepts and returns a fixed type, and to test it out.

issue_title: Contract method not incrementing as expected
issue_body:
* Version: 3.16.5
* Python: 3.6
* OS: linux
### What was wrong?

I have some solidity functions: 
  - function Voting(bytes32[] candidateNames)
  - totalVotesFor(bytes32 candidate) returns (uint8)
  - voteForCandidate(bytes32 candidate)

Full `Voting.sol` file is available [here](https://github.com/llSourcell/Your_First_Decentralized_Application/blob/master/voting.sol)

From Python
```python
import json
import web3
from web3 import Web3, HTTPProvider, TestRPCProvider
from solc import compile_source
from web3.contract import ConciseContract
  
contract_file = 'Voting.sol'
with open(contract_file, 'r') as myfile:
    code = myfile.read()
compiled_sol = compile_source(code)
contract_interface = compiled_sol['<stdin>:Voting']
w3 = Web3(TestRPCProvider(port=8546))
# Instantiate and deploy contract
bytecode = contract_interface['bin']
contract = w3.eth.contract(abi=contract_interface['abi'], bytecode=bytecode)
candidates = [b'Rama', b'Niki', b'Jose']
tx_hash = contract.deploy(args=[candidates], transaction={'from': w3.eth.accounts[0], 'gas': 410000})
# Get tx receipt to get contract address
tx_receipt = w3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
contract_address = tx_receipt['contractAddress']
  
# Contract instance in concise mode
abi = contract_interface['abi']
contract_instance = w3.eth.contract(address=contract_address, abi=abi, ContractFactoryClass=ConciseContract)
  
print('Validating the Rama candidate')
print(contract_instance.validCandidate('Rama'))
print('Validating fake candidates are not allowed')
print(contract_instance.validCandidate('Fake'))
print('Total votes for Rama')
print(contract_instance.totalVotesFor('Rama'))
print('Add 1 vote for Rama')
contract_instance.voteForCandidate('Rama')
print('Total votes for Rama')
print(contract_instance.totalVotesFor('Rama'))
```

I don't get any error message but the number of votes for Rama is not incremented (it remains 0). I also tested with `b'Rama'` instead of `'Rama'` without more success.

Output: 
```
Validating the Rama candidate
127.0.0.1 - - [21/Mar/2018 09:23:17] "POST / HTTP/1.1" 200 107
/home/jbp/miniconda3/envs/ether-contract/lib/python3.6/site-packages/eth_abi/abi.py:92: DeprecationWarning: Automatic inference of hex encoded data has been deprecated. Please adjust your code to ensure that the data argument for `decode_single` is a byte string
  "Automatic inference of hex encoded data has been deprecated. "
True
Validating fake candidate are not allowed
127.0.0.1 - - [21/Mar/2018 09:23:17] "POST / HTTP/1.1" 200 107
False
Total votes for Rama
127.0.0.1 - - [21/Mar/2018 09:23:17] "POST / HTTP/1.1" 200 107
0
Add 1 vote for Rama
127.0.0.1 - - [21/Mar/2018 09:23:17] "POST / HTTP/1.1" 200 43
Total votes for Rama
127.0.0.1 - - [21/Mar/2018 09:23:17] "POST / HTTP/1.1" 200 107
0
```
Any idea why ?

issue_title: Broken and redundant functions in mrs.query
issue_body:
Most functions in mrs.query have either become redundant or were not kept up-to-date with core changes:

- [x] `select_nodeids()` - broken
- [x] `select_nodes()` - broken
- [x] `select_eps()` - broken
- [x] `select_args()` - broken
- [x] `select_links()` - broken
- [x] `select_hcons()` - broken
- [x] `select_icons()` - broken
- [x] `find_argument_target()` - broken
- [x] `find_quantifier()` - broken and redundant (`Xmrs.nodeid(...,quantifier=True)`)
- [x] `get_outbound_args()` - broken and redundant (`Xmrs.outgoing_args()`)
- [x] `nodeid()` - redundant (`Xmrs.nodeid()`)

This module has no unit tests, so they should be added for the above and also for the following:

- [x] `find_subgraphs_by_pred()`

As per the title, conda currently only knows about the usual x86, x86_64 and armv7l architectures. Since aarch64 systems are compatible with armhf binaries, conda just needs to alias 'aarch64' to 'armv7l'.

The only hitch involved in approaching the problem via such an alias is that the armhf linker (ld-linux-armhf) may not be installed - its a rare situation currently, since most ARM systems opt for a 64bit kernel/32 bit userspace due to the rarity of 64 bit ARM binaries. The user (with sudo privileges) would need to install the linker in such a situation.

See [PR 4809](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4809) for implementation.

See https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/2886

issue_title: conda libtcl8.5.so conflicts with system libtcl8.5.so
issue_body:
We use environment modules on our HPC to manage package installations. When I have a miniconda environment installed, however, it breaks the modules command, e.g.:

```
$ module avail
init.c(556):ERROR:161: Cannot initialize TCL
warn UNKNOWN command:  test 0 = 1
```

When I look at the linked library information I get the following:

```
ldd /apps/Modules/default/bin/modulecmd.wrapped
	linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007fffb0bdd000)
	libtcl8.5.so => /home/gawbul/miniconda2/lib/libtcl8.5.so (0x00007fba595fb000)
	libdl.so.2 => /lib64/libdl.so.2 (0x000000383f800000)
	libm.so.6 => /lib64/libm.so.6 (0x000000383f400000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib64/libc.so.6 (0x000000383f000000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x000000383ec00000)
```

However, when I aren't in the conda environment I get this:
```
ldd /apps/Modules/default/bin/modulecmd.wrapped
	linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007ffc9d9e8000)
	libtcl8.5.so => /usr/lib64/libtcl8.5.so (0x0000003840400000)
	libdl.so.2 => /lib64/libdl.so.2 (0x000000383f800000)
	libm.so.6 => /lib64/libm.so.6 (0x000000383f400000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib64/libc.so.6 (0x000000383f000000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x000000383ec00000)
```

If I do `LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH ldd /apps/Modules/default/bin/modulecmd.wrapped` it fixes it, but I don't know what impact this will have on any of the conda applications currently.

Obviously `libtcl8.5.so` is conflicting.

issue_title: Conda unnecessarily re-downloaded all of Anaconda 4.3
issue_body:
I installed Anaconda 4.3, Python 3.6 variant, from scratch on my system a week ago.

```bash
$ python -V

$ ls -Fla `which python`

$ ls -Flad /Users/ijstokes/anaconda
drwxr-xr-x  26 ijstokes  staff  884 Feb  9 17:54 /Users/ijstokes/anaconda/
```

(see versions of Python, Anaconda, and time stamp)

Just now I wanted to create a "virgin" Anaconda 4.3 environment.  What would I expect to happen?  Provided there haven't been any patch releases (4.3.1) in the past week I'd expect this to take about 5 seconds as all the packages are already available from my original install (part of the root environment). issue_title: conda update --all killed a Win7 64bit installation of anaconda that worked previously
issue_body:
Background: 
* I had installed Anaconda months ago with a default of Python 3.5 and additional environment of Python 2.7.  This was done on a Windows 7 64 bit laptop w/ 1.4 Ghz processor, 16 Gigs of Ram and ample HD space
* Spyder stopped working for no reason.  only known change between then and now:  Windows updates.
* in attempting to debug this, ran across something that suggested maybe I needed to update my Python 3.5 environment in which it was originally deployed

What happened
* ran "conda update --all" on my 3.5 Python environment
* now that environment is missing from my Jupyter notebook menus 
  ** original environment before uddate blew it away:  
       *** two 3.5 menu items for root 3.5 env:  Python [default] and Python [root]
       *** following normal default install created these menu items for same single environment
       *** after update messed it up, only my Python [Py27] env that I added in post install remained
* and all icons relating to the default 3.5 environment are gone
* and the activate.bat and activate script and missing from the Anaconda3 default env folder
* and anaconda command prompt does not work now (error says the batch file is missing)
* it appears I now cannot use my Anaconda3 env - only the secondary Python 27 env is working

What I would like from you
* Please look into what caused this error.  It is a bad user experience when you take a step that should ensure your environment is up to date and instead it kills your environment

* additional symptoms and notes:
  ** as is documented below, "conda update --all" when ran, produced these error lines at the end of update after updating all or most of my packages:
------------------
Linking packages ...
PaddingError: Placeholder of length '30' too short in package qt-5.6.2-vc14_0.%
The package must be rebuilt with conda-build > 2.0.
-------------------
  ** in addition to all symptoms mentioned above, it seems that when this went bad, it took out my right-click hot menu associations for Git bash (I had git installed on my computer as well).

In the interim:
* problems with Spyder, this bad experience with 3.5, and an upcoming class that will be taught in 2.7 have convinced me to re-install my whole environment this time as a 2.7 distribution with an extra 3.x environment.  I may try 3.6 that I think is out instead of 3.5 - but this behavior is worrisome as it indicates the update command can kill a working distribution.

Here are all the messages from running of "conda update --all" on my environment showing what it did up until the problems occurred:

The package must be rebuilt with conda-build > 2.0.

`conda remove --all` used to remove an environment if it existed, and quietly succeed if it was already gone. Now this command fails when the environment does not exist. This makes something formerly easy to do in an automated way now difficult especially in a cross-platform way on Windows.

```
C:\>conda remove --all -n nonenv
CondaEnvironmentNotFoundError: Could not find environment: nonenv .
You can list all discoverable environments with `conda info --envs`.



C:\>echo %ERRORLEVEL%
1
```

Just suppressing the error is not appealing, because there are many error modes different from the environment not existing that should trigger a failure.

The help message output appears to be self-contradictory. While I believe dependencies are suggested to be updated by default, the help message output for the optional flags `--update-deps` and `--no-update-deps` appear to contradict each other.

    $ conda --version
    conda 4.2.13
    $ conda install --help
    ...
    --update-dependencies, --update-deps
                          Don't update dependencies (default: True).
    --no-update-dependencies, --no-update-deps`
                          Don't update dependencies (default: False).

issue_title: Wrong read coordinates and wrong TLEN after Fetching alignment record from a BAM file
issue_body:
@nlhepler 
Hi Pysam team,
I just installed  version pysam==0.9.1.4, and see the following problem while capturing the read lines. The CHROM and POS for both the read and its mate are shifted by one (1) from the original line in the BAM file. So the **columns 3, 4, 7 and 8 are wrong**. 
Furthermore the TLEN or column 9 is totally different from the original value. In the example below you can see that the original value is -872 and was converted to 57.

But, while using the code x.next or x.rnext, the value are correct, using the code x.start or x.next_start give the wrong coordinates. (see code below for details).

import pysam

    print("x.rname \t" + str(bamf.getrname(x.rname)))

issue_title: get_aligned_pairs semantics changed. was this intentional?
Runtime dll's are not copied next to the executable anymore on Windows
issue_body:
OCIDE used to copy runtime dlls next to the compiled executable so that user can run the executable directly from the file explorer.

@ arcanjoebc1971 reported by email that this does not work anymore in v4.6.x. The issue is that now that the full compiler path is used, the glob expression used to collect the needed dlls does not work anymore. 

The solution is to use the dirname of the compiler path in the glob expression

However, using the portal to provision acs does the proper registrations.

Expected:
az acs create does the same steps as the portal arm template for registering Microsoft.Networking and any other resources required.

issue_title: "Az Storage Blob Download .. -Max-Connections 1" file handling needs improvement.
issue_body:
Currently the "AZ Storage Blob Download ... --Max-connections 1" arguments will only attempt to pull the larger files in one peice.  When the file it is trying to download is larger than the memory on the workstation running the task the process consumes the workstation's entire memory and swap file then fails.

Looking to get the Azure CLI Storage blob download command structure to handle "--Max-Connections 1" like it does when the max connections is set to 2 or more.  That is to handle files larger than the memory of the workstation by breaking the file into multiple peices and make multiple requests to get the full file to download.


The Customer needs to download the blob to a named pipe so they must be able to use a single download connection to prevent seek operations on the pipe.

issue_title: `az network application-gateway frontend-port create` takes a long time to finish
issue_body:
We're trying to create an application gateway that has multiple ports open (one for HTTP and one for HTTPS)

Currently we have set up the gateway using the old azure client, with args like this
```
azure network application-gateway create [...] --http-listener-protocol https --frontend-port 443
```

When we try to automate creating an additional listener on this gateway, creating a `frontend-port` seems to take forever:

```
$ az network application-gateway frontend-port create [...] -name frontendPort02 --port 80 --verbose
Starting long running operation 'Starting network application-gateway frontend-port create'
```

If we do an `az network application-gateway frontend-port list`, we see the port has been created but the `provisioningState` is `Updating`, even after something like 10-20 minutes.  It takes more than 20 minutes for the state to move to finished.  

Are we doing this wrong?  Is there another way we could be doing this?  Is there a way we could create multiple front-end listeners when we create an application gateway?

Is it actually not finished until after 20 minutes, or is the API being extra conservative about reporting something in 'Updating' vs 'Succeeded' state?
Try out the SDK change at https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/1569

issue_title: Transmat normalization leads to incorrect adaptation (left-right HMMs)
issue_body:
Hello again

It seems to me that the problem related to #33 and #65 still exists. Transmat adaptation in left-right HMMs results in ergodic topology (zero probabilities are raised to non-zero).

in `base.py` we have:

    def _do_mstep(self, stats):
        (...)
        # The ``np.where`` calls guard against updating forbidden states
        # or transitions in e.g. a left-right HMM.
        (...)
        if 't' in self.params:
            transmat_ = self.transmat_prior - 1.0 + stats['trans']
            self.transmat_ = np.where(self.transmat_ == 0.0,
                                      self.transmat_, transmat_)
            ############################
            normalize(self.transmat_, axis=1)  # WE NORMALIZE AFTER CHECKING FOR ZEROS
            ############################

And in `utils.py` we have:
```
def normalize(a, axis=None):
    (...)
    ####################
    a += np.finfo(float).eps  # AND HERE WE LOOSE OUR "GUARD"
    ####################

    a_sum = a.sum(axis)
    if axis and a.ndim > 1:
        # Make sure we don't divide by zero.
        a_sum[a_sum == 0] = 1
        shape = list(a.shape)
        shape[axis] = 1
        a_sum.shape = shape

    a /= a_sum

    # TODO: should return nothing, since the operation is inplace.
    return a
```

## Expected behaviour

Passing the URL, get a not empty response

## Actual behaviour

## Steps to reproduce

```
import asyncio
import aiohttp

url1 = 'https://www.jonathanadler.com/art/by-artist/slim-aarons/slim-aarons-andldquo%3Bverbier-vacationandrdquo%3B-photograph/19135.html'

async def request(url):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get(url, allow_redirects=True) as response:
            return response, await response.text()

async def check(url):
    response, text = await request(url)
    print(response.history)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(check(url1))
```

        async for row in await cursor:
            print(row)
```
or not use async for loop but that defeats the purpose of async.

issue_title: devtools can not work with session[aioredis]
issue_body:
* **aiohttp-devtools version:**
0.6.4
* **aiohttp version:**
2.3.4
* **python version:**
3.6.3
* **Platform:**
OSX

### Issue Summary

Starting with `adev runserver simple.py` will raise `RuntimeError: This event loop is already running` but it works for `python simple.py`

### Code Example

```python
#simple.py
from aiohttp import web
import asyncio
import aioredis
from aiohttp_session import setup, get_session
from aiohttp_session.redis_storage import RedisStorage


async def create_redis_pool():
    return await aioredis.create_pool(('127.0.0.1', 6379))


def setup_sessions(app, pool):
    storage = RedisStorage(pool)
    setup(app, storage)


async def index(request):
    sess = await get_session(request)
    last_visit = sess['last_visit'] if 'last_visit' in sess else "never"
    sess['last_visit'] = "yes"
    return web.Response(text=last_visit)


def create_app():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    pool = loop.run_until_complete(create_redis_pool())
    app = web.Application()
    setup_sessions(app, pool)
    app.router.add_get("/", index)
    return app


if __name__ == '__main__':
    web.run_app(create_app(), port=8000)
```

### Steps to reproduce
1. `adev runserver simple.py`.
2. check out error message.

In a given assignment, the grad students have to do all of the problems, and the undergrads have to do most but not all of the problems. I saw in issue #488 that I could assign an additional hw or maybe have a separate notebook for the extra problems that only the grad students do, but then is there a way to have two student groups in my config that I assign a little differently? Or maybe that is a different way that would be better? Just wondering what my options here might be. Thanks.

issue_title: Packaging - the custom msg, entry points, and cached static assets solution.
issue_body:
Since we haven't agreed on this yet, I'm opening an issue instead of writing an IPEP.  If this is agreed on, I'll write an IPEP.

The week before last, @ellisonbg and I brainstormed about Jupyter packaging.  As I remember it, the best solution we came up with requires a combination of Python packaging entry-points, a new message type, and static asset caching (in the web server).  This is my understanding of how this solution would work (my notes from our meeting are at home, and my apartment is being fumigated, so I don't have access to them).

## Jupyter level, kernel extensions
![screen shot 2015-05-26 at 1 47 13 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3292874/7822986/f5dba636-03ad-11e5-8de4-8d559bd46acb.png)
A new message, the first, (in blue) would be added, allowing the server to ask the kernel if the static assets it knows about, associated with that kernel, is correct.  The message would be a dict of static asset path and contents hashes.

The same message in the opposite direction is the kernel's response.  It would be some type of data structure, maybe a binary message, containing static asset paths and their contents, and a list of the static assets that can be deleted from the cache.

A second new message (in red), would be added that would allow the kernel to invoke a require.js call in the front-end.  This would eliminate the need of a notebook extensions list, and it's need to be configured.  This is preferred over standard display(JS) calls, because the notebook contents will remain unaffected.

## IPython level, kernel extensions
Python entry points will be used as a registry.  Two entry points will be defined:  

 1. an entry point for code to run when the kernel is started.
 2. an entry point for a method that returns static assets (paths).

## Jupyter level, server extensions and notebook extensions
Python entry points will be used as a registry.  Three entry points will be defined:  

 1. an entry point for code to run when the server is started.
 2. an entry point for a method that returns static assets (paths).
 3. an entry point for a paths to be `require`ed when the notebook page loads.

Apologies for this somewhat long issue. It describes my progress (and ultimate failure) to install jupyter extensions.

I just upgraded from ipython to jupyter and am trying to get my environment working properly. I upgraded using conda (``conda install jupyter``) and have jupyer 1.0.0 installed.I am trying to get extensions working, following these instructions:
https://github.com/ipython-contrib/IPython-notebook-extensions/wiki/Home_Jupyter

First I was stuck on installing extensions:
https://github.com/ipython-contrib/IPython-notebook-extensions/wiki/Home_Jupyter#1-installing-an-extension

When I first tried

```bash
 jupyter nbextension install usability/codefolding/main --user
```

I got a long stack trace ending with
```
IOError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: u'usability/codefolding/main
```

It seemed like it was looking for an extension in my local file path, so I surmised I was supposed to clone the IPython-notebook-extensions repo and install the extensions from the base directory.

``` bash
git clone git@github.com:ipython-contrib/IPython-notebook-extensions.git
cd IPython-notebook-extensions
jupyter nbextension install usability/codefolding/main --user
```

Still got the IOError. However, examining the repo files, I was able to finally get something to work
``` bash
jupyter nbextension install usability/codefolding/main.js --user
```
This command completed without an error, so I assumed it was working. In fact, it put the file ``main.js`` into the path ``/Users/rpa/Library/Jupyter/nbextensions``, which I presume is not the desired behavior.

After futher trial and error, I ended up with
``` bash
$ jupyter nbextension install usability/codefolding --user
```

issue_title: Differentiating chart based dynamic programming algorithms
issue_body:
Consider the following code, it sums up the entries in y by keeping a running sum in the chart at `x[0, 0]` and finally outputs the value at chart position `x[0, 0]` when the loop finishes. This is a /mwe/ and more complicated logic is used in algorithms like inside-outside for CKY parsing or forward-backward for finding the sum of all valid paths in a linear chain CRF. I was wondering if it was possible to fix the library to get gradients for such chart based algorithms ? 
```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
def assignment(y, x):
    for i in range(3):
        x[0, 0] = np.sum(np.array([y[i], x[0, 0]]))
    return x[0, 0]

def test_assignment():
    x = np.zeros((1,1))
    f = lambda y : assignment(y, x)
    y = np.random.rand(3,)
    print f(y)
    print grad(f)(y)
    return

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_assignment()

>>> 0.530750805709
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_autograd.py", line 35, in <module>
    test_assignment()
  File "test_autograd.py", line 31, in test_assignment
    print grad(f)(y)
  File "~/site-packages/autograd/core.py", line 16, in gradfun
    end_node = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "test_autograd.py", line 28, in <lambda>
    f = lambda y : assignment(y, x)
  File "test_autograd.py", line 23, in assignment
    x[0, 0] = np.max(np.array([y[i], x[0, 0]]))
TypeError: float() argument must be a string or a number

issue_title: Is it really a good idea for the imports list to be separate?
issue_body:
This is a consistent question I get when I demo peru for people. Why not put the import path for a module in the module's declaration? There are two decent reasons and one bad reason:

1. It's nice to be able to look in one place and see everything that peru is going to do when you sync.
2. Imports are not necessarily one-module-one-path. A module could be imported multiple times with different rules.
3. Bad reason: this is a holdover from when remote modules were more involved, with potentially their own imports, when some symmetry between the remote modules and the toplevel module made sense.

Allowing import paths as part of a module declaration definitely simplifies the hello world example. I could go either way on examples that are more complicated than that. I *really* want to avoid having two different ways to do the same thing, like allowing both an imports list and inline import paths. I think the biggest question for me right now is whether point (1) is really true, or whether I just think it's true because I'm used to it...

I'm starting a list of release notes for Blockstack 0.14.1. Please add things by leaving a comment and I'll edit this post to include the items. I will then expand on the features/updates and give more details. 

- [ ] New REST API
- [ ] CLI integrated with Blockstack Core for simplicity 
- [ ] Resolver integrated with Blockstack Core 
- [ ] Search integrated with Blockstack Core

Registering Blockstack names requires broadcasting a number of bitcoin transactions and waiting between them. Transactions need to be broadcast within certain intervals, otherwise the name registration will fail.

This is fine when a registration is performed on a server which is always running and connected to the network.

It poses a problem for registrations conducted by end users using Blockstack Portal because most of them use machines that are not always on and many use laptops which can have limited power and intermittent network connectivity. 

Name registration will fail if the user is unable to broadcast the transactions at the right time.

This can happen in several scenarios:
1. The user turns her computer off after submitting the registration and doesn't turn it back on and restart Blockstack within a certain window of time (~12 hours)
2. The user's computer loses network connectivity and is unable to broadcast the transactions at the right time.

One way to solve this is to create an optional service where a user can generate transactions in advance and send them to a trusted 3rd broadcast service that queues them up and sends them out at the appropriate time.

We'd make this on by default in Blockstack for Mac and likely other end user platforms. Advanced users should be able to disable this feature in which their local machine would be responsible for broadcasting the transactions at the proper time. They should also be able to point it to alternative deployments of the service.

There should be two API endpoints: 1 for submitting the transactions and another for checking their status in the broadcast queue.

Tagging @muneeb-ali @shea256 @kantai @jcnelson for additional input.

issue_title: noop with no changes reporting as 'noop' instead of 'unchanged' for puppetdb > 4.2.0
issue_body:
Scenario: puppet is running as --noop every 30 minutes, and so far they were being reported in puppetboard as 'unchanged'; which is exactly what I expected, as no 'noops' events existed. It was pypuppetdb 0.3.1, puppetdb 4.0.x. 


Something example code to reproduce it:
```
from pypuppetdb import connect

puppetdb = connect(host='puppetdb', port=8080, timeout=20,)
nodes = puppetdb.nodes(with_status=True)
for node in nodes:
  print(node.name + “” + puppet.status)
```

Looking at https://github.com/voxpupuli/pypuppetdb/blob/master/pypuppetdb/api.py#L413, it appears that I was getting in the 'else' clause, because 'latest_report_noop' was only included in puppetdb [4.1.0](https://docs.puppet.com/puppetdb/latest/release_notes.html#section-6):

```
                    if node['latest_report_noop']:
                        node['status'] = 'noop'
                    else:
                        node['status'] = node['latest_report_status']
```


I upgraded puppetdb from 4.0.x to 4.2.x. And suddenly now those same nodes are reporting as 'noop' regardless if there are changes or not to be applied. I suppose you wouldn't expect such change of behaviour, and in all fairness, puppetboard does treat 'noop' as 'pending changes'. 

Shouldn't it be using `latest_report_noop_pending` instead of `latest_report_noop`?



In case it helps, an example output in puppetdb 4.2.x, after noop without changes. 
```
  {
    "deactivated": null,
    "latest_report_hash": "02f848510cab4b519a58d3698ef9b07459914f44",

issue_body: I moved pipeline inputs into cgl-pipeline-inputs, so we do not need project specific buckets anymore.

Misc issues:

* We sort, index, resort, reindex.
* Disk settings aren't given properly in `adam_gatk_pipeline`, so we OOD.

Check if the changes introduced in https://github.com/log2timeline/dfvfs/commit/e694f3c615dff2d07ac2d637587ede381d1587b8 fully support Windows. Also see: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/joachimmetz/dfvfs/build/210

The `RHCIDeployment` entity defines its fields liks so:

```python
            'name': entity_fields.StringField(required=True),
            'organization_id': entity_fields.IntegerField(required=True),
            'lifecycle_environment_id': entity_fields.IntegerField(
                required=True),
            'deploy_rhev': entity_fields.BooleanField(required=True),
            'rhev_engine_host_id': entity_fields.IntegerField(required=True),
            'rhev_storage_type': entity_fields.StringField(required=True),
            'rhev_engine_admin_password': entity_fields.StringField(),
        }
```

There's two issues here:

* "_id" should not be appended to the end of field names. "_id", "_ids", "s" and other suffixes are all appended to field names in certain contexts (GETting, PUTting, POSTing). The base field names should not have *any* suffix.
* `IntegerField`s are used to describe foreign key relationships. That's totally wrong.

        db = await db_setup
        with await db._pool as redis:

            print(res)
            assert res, "PONG"

I recently updated python packages, including this one, and ran across a potential issue with the remote driver. If I run the pytest command with `--host notlocalhost --driver Remote --port 1234` (just an example) it does not work. If I run the command with `--driver Remote --port 1234 --host notlocalhost` it does work. For the command with the host first, the host seems to become the default `localhost` instead of the provided host `notlocalhost`.

The actual error due to the incorrect host is: `E urllib.error.URLError: <urlopen error [Errno 111] Connection refused>`

I updated pytest-selenium from 1.9.1 to 1.11.2. Using 1.9.1 I did not see this error.

Where as rc1-update1 works fine. If there is open bug around this, could you please tell me last RC2 build which works.

This can easily be reproduced if you create a container using 
    docker run -i -t debian:jessie
and isntall DNVM on it and run dnvm upgrade -r coreclr -u

and execute dnx --version

Please help!

issue_title: Running same command again always creates new folder instead of updating
issue_body:
I thought the certificates should be updated when letsencrypt is run with the same command line again. But currently I'm experiencing the following:
~~~
~/letsencrypt # ./letsencrypt-auto --config /etc/letsencrypt/cli.ini -d <DOMAIN> certonly
Updating letsencrypt and virtual environment dependencies.......
Running with virtualenv: /root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt --config /etc/letsencrypt/cli.ini -d <DOMAIN> certonly

   /etc/letsencrypt/live/<DOMAIN>/fullchain.pem. Your cert will

   the future, simply run Let's Encrypt again.

   Donating to ISRG / Let's Encrypt:   https://letsencrypt.org/donate
   Donating to EFF:                    https://eff.org/donate-le
~~~

After the second run the certificates are not updated but a new folder **\<DOMAIN\>-0001** is created. If I run it a third time **\<DOMAIN\>-0002** is created and so on.
I have checked out the current master af68109db3f23d85fa3fcc6853b57fda6c35a9ee.

I have my apache2 server running behind an openvpn server, which passes trough anything not vpn related. the problem is that when I run let's encrypt with the apache plugin, it messes with my ports.conf. So I first have to stop openvpn, then start the let's encrypt script. after that, I have to cleanup my configs so it'l have port 4545 for ssl instead of 443. Then I need to restart apache2 and after that I can start my vpn again. I really don't want to write a script that does all of the above.

Will there be  or is there a way to specify costum ports for the apache2 configs beforehand?

FYI:  RHEL 7 comes with python 2.7.5.

Neither my Ubuntu nor Debian machines had "dialog" installed. Found out it was a dependency via a Python error messages mentioning a missing executable. Not sure where in the docs it would be appropriate.

issue_title: TypeError when sending to an umlaut domain
issue_body:
One of our older Python 2.7 projects ran into an error when sending a transactional email to a domain containing an umlaut (e.g. "someone@my-umlaut-domäin.com"). Our transmission includes recipient attributes:

```
sp.transmissions.send(
    recipients=[{
        'address': {
            'name': u'Firstname Lastname',
            'email': u'someone@my-umlaut-domäin.com',
        }
    }],
    template='my-template'
)
```

The package version 1.1.1 gave us the following error:
`At least one valid recipient is required`

Updating to the latest release 1.3.2 returned this error:
`TypeError: sequence item 0: expected string, dict found`

Unicode recipients should be supported since package version 1.0.3. Is there something we miss?

We need to add some options that are available via API:

* inline_images
* ip_pool

issue_title: Python consumer doesn't pick up messages from auto created topic
issue_body:
I can't seem to figure out how to get a Python Consumer to receive messages from a topic that's auto-created after it starts polling. If I start both a Python Consumer and a kafka-console-consumer for the same topic that doesn't exist yet, then use a Python Producer to produce a message to that topic, the kafka-console-consumer gets the message, but the Python Consumer never does. I have to start the Python Consumer after the topic exists to receive messages.

My consumer is basically the example High-level Consumer in the README.

```
consumer = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'kafka:9092', 'group.id': 'pssync'})
consumer.subscribe(['pssync-transactions'])
running = True
while running:
    message = consumer.poll(timeout=30)
    if not message:
        continue
    elif not message.error():
        print(message.value())
    elif message.error().code() != KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
        print(message.error())
        running = False
```

The console-consumer is being run like this.

```
kafka-console-consumer --topic pssync-transactions --zookeeper zookeeper:2181
```

Is this a limitation of the confluent-kafka-python Consumer, or am I doing something wrong?

issue_title: Segfault after calling a closed consumer
issue_body:
Description
===========

Once a Consumer has been closed, calling any subsequent method references the inter handle `self->rk` which is now `NULL` and causes a segfault.

The consumer should instead raise an exception for the application code to handle. 

How to reproduce
================

```
c = Consumer()
c.close()
c.assignment() # SEGFAULT
```
This would make pypiserver useful for serving a private (proprietary) package repository.

issue_title: InitialWorkDir contents must be read-only by default
issue_body:
Related conformance test: https://github.com/common-workflow-language/common-workflow-language/issues/591

Implementation hints:

- before staging, record the permission bits of the files
- try to change the permission bits of the files recursively to be read-only for all users, the file's group, the file's user

(this is the equivalent of:
```
chmod -R a-r
```
and if that fails
```
chmod -R o-r
chmod  -R g-r
chmod -R u-r
```
)
- after the step execution, write back the permission bits.

issue_title: dockerPull field in DockerRequirement should pull image updates before running
issue_body: I have been running some CWL workflows that utilize the DockerRequirement "dockerPull" field, and when the Docker image in question is not present locally, the field works as intended, pulling the image first before running. However, if the image is updated in the Docker registry sometime later, the "dockerPull" field will continue to run the local image with no actual attempt to do a "docker pull" for updates. This requires me to have to do a manual "docker pull" on the command line before invoking the CWL. Is it possible to have CWL perform an actual "docker pull" every time the workflow is invoked with the "dockerPull" field (to grab the latest image update if available), as opposed to simply doing a "docker run" if the image already exists locally?

issue_title: write Integer data to serialPort raises Error on Linux
issue_body:
I found a bug and I am not well aware on how to upload a patch (just didn't do that before and don't know the workflow).

In file serialposix.py in function `write(self, data)` the data is transformed to bytes:
(line 518) `d = to_bytes(data)`
but at the end (line 558) it `return len(data)`, what fails if data is an integer
it should `return len(d)`
(This error does not occur on windows because line 308 in serialwin32.py just does `data = to_bytes(data)`)

python(python 3.4.4 version 32 )
```python
port= serial.Serial()
>>> port
Serial<id=0x2a706b0, open=False>(port=None, baudrate=9600, bytesize=8, parity='N', stopbits=1, timeout=None, xonxoff=False, rtscts=False, dsrdtr=False)
>>> port.setPort('com5')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'Serial' object has no attribute 'setPort'
```
But there is no problem python 3.4.4 64bit

In MPF we use pyserial-asyncio for serials with up to 6MBaud/s. Unfortunately, we fail to saturate the serial while sending as soon as the asyncio loop is not completely idle. This is mostly caused by very small serial buffers in the linux kernel (1024 bytes by default) which can only be adjusted at compile time. Saturating a 6M serial with a 1024 byte buffer requires nearly 6.000 wake-ups with very low jitter which is the reason why this will never work properly.

To solve this we use separate threads (using asyncio's run_in_executor). Would you be up to support that in pyserial-asyncio? I would port our sender code to pyserial-asyncio in that case.

I understand there’s some trade offs with accuracy to consider, but my naive mind seems to think Turi Create could automatically quantize it based on the data to achieve the best results. Thanks!

When using `qsub` with SGE, there is a flag `-b` that specifies whether to treat the command as a binary or script. if `-b y`, `qsub` will assume the command is available on the compute hosts (and not necessarily the submission host) whereas if `-b n`, the file will be copied over from the submission.

 Is there a way to do that in `drmaaa` or should I use either `nativeSpecification` or something like `transferFiles`?

Full text from man page:


       -b y[es]|n[o]
              Available for qsub, qrsh only. Qalter does not allow changing this option. This option cannot be embedded in the script file itself.

              Gives  the  user the possibility to indicate explicitly whether command should be treated as binary or script. If the argument of -b is ’y’, then command  may be a binary or script.  The command might
              not be accessible from the submission host.  Nothing except the path of the command will be transferred from the submission host to the execution host. Path aliasing will be applied  to  the  path  of
              command before it is executed.

              If  the  argument  of  -b is ’n’ then command needs to be a script, and it will be handled as such. The script file has to be accessible by the submission host. It will be transferred to the execution
              host. qsub/qrsh will search directive prefixes within scripts.

              qsub will implicitly use -b n, whereas qrsh will apply the -b y option if nothing else is specified.

              The value specified with this option or the corresponding value specified in qmon will only be passed to defined JSV instances if the value is yes.  The name of the parameter will be b. The value will
              be y also when the long form yes was specified during submission.  (See -jsv option below or find more information concerning JSV in jsv(1).)

              Please  note  that submission of command as a script (-b n) can have a significant performance impact, especially for short running jobs and big job scripts.  Script submission adds a number of opera-
              tions to the submission process: The job script needs to be
              - parsed at client side (for special comments)
              - transferred from submit client to qmaster
              - spooled in qmaster
              - transferred to execd at job execution
              - spooled in execd
              - removed from spooling both in execd and qmaster once the job is done
              If job scripts are available on the execution nodes, e.g. via NFS, binary submission can be the better choice.

issue_title: ForecastModel.set_query_latlon provides arguments in the wrong order to query.lonlat_box. Longitude should precede latitude.
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
Forecast.set_query_latlon provides arguments in the wrong order to query.lonlat_box. Longitude should precede latitude. 

**To Reproduce**
~~~
from pvlib.forecast import GFS

model = GFS()
model.connect_to_catalog()
model.longitude = [10, 350]
model.latitude = [-80, 80]
model.set_query_latlon()
print(model.query.spatial_query)
~~~
returns
~~~
OrderedDict([('west', -80), ('east', 80), ('south', 10), ('north', 350)])
~~~
**Expected behavior**
~~~
OrderedDict([('west', 10), ('east', 350), ('south', -80), ('north', 80)])
~~~
and this becomes an issue with GET requests when `get_data` is called... `&west=-80&east=350&south=-80&north=80`

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: `0.5.2`
 - ``pandas.__version__``: `0.23.0`
 - python: `Python 3.6.5 :: Anaconda, Inc.`

issue_title: Strings that should not be argparsed as different python literals
issue_body:
Updating a string valued config parameter with e.g. "123" turns the string parameter into an integer. This might lead to unexpected behaviour. One could manually convert it back to a string, but it would be nicer if _sacred_ had a way to handle this automatically.
Differentiating `python command with param=123` and `python command with param="123"` would be a nice solution, but unfortunately the python _argparse_ never actually sees the quotation marks and therefore can not treat them differently.

Is there maybe a different work around or something I am forgetting?

issue_title: Add Support for additional VPC with dedicated Subnet
issue_body:
Sevenseconds sollte dahingehend erweitert werden, dass auch zuständliche VPCs angelegt werden können.

Diese VPC werden unter anderem benötigt für VPN oder DirectConnect Verbindungen.

Als Option muss das Private-Subnetz und optional eine IP für ein Customer-VPN-Gateway übergeben werden.

Wenn eine IP für ein Customer-VPN-Gateway übergeben wurde, dann soll zusätzlich ein VPN angelegt werden.

Mittels Option soll auch die Wahl zwischen "private only", "default VPC config" und "default VPC without NAT" bestehen

* **private only**: nur ein Internal-Subnetze pro AZ, ohne Nat-Instanzen und Internet-GW
* **default VPC config**: ein internal Subnetz pro AZ, ein DMZ-Subnetz pro AZ, Nat-Instanz pro DMZ-Netz, Routing-Table pro internal Subnetz, Internet Gateway
* **default VPC without NAT**: ein internal Subnetz pro AZ, ein DMZ-Subnetz pro AZ, Internet Gateway
...not only for used regions

issue_title: Model.getConstInterp "invalid argument" Z3Exception instead of null return value
issue_body:
According to the Z3 Java API [documentation](http://z3prover.github.io/api/html/classcom_1_1microsoft_1_1z3_1_1_model.html#aceef5d8c0c6804f696668ac5cc48d850), if a constant does not have an interpretation in a model, `Model.getConstInterp(Expr)` should return `null`. However, I get an "invalid argument" `Z3Exception` on the latest master (0286cee45026a1cf97ae9f001b4049496267f691) build on Windows. This also holds for `Model.getConstInterp(FuncDecl)` This behavior is reproducible with the following code.

`final Context context = new Context();`
`final Solver solver = context.mkSimpleSolver();`

`final BoolExpr a = context.mkBoolConst("a");`
`final BoolExpr b = context.mkBoolConst("b");`
`final BoolExpr expr = context.mkOr(a, b);`

`solver.add(expr);`
`solver.check();`
`final Model model = solver.getModel();`

`System.out.println(model.getConstInterp(a)); // value of 'a' is 'true'`
`System.out.println(model.getConstInterp(b)); // Z3Exception: invalid argument`

issue_title: String contains substring validity times out
issue_body:
Hello, 

I'm adding strings to Rosette. I currently am working on some simplifications. One simplification that I make is that a string should always contain all of its substrings. 

I'm testing my simplifications against Z3 to make sure we don't disagree on anything. This simplification works in Z3 with a bounded length, but currently times out for me (I wait a few minutes) when I try with an unbounded length. I understand validity in some fragments of the theory of strings is undecidable with unbounded lengths, but I think this case is simple enough that it might be worth considering separately. 

If it isn't, or if you try not to special-case too many things, then feel free to ignore this.

Here's the query: 

```
(set-option :model_validate true)

(declare-fun c0 () String)
(declare-fun c1 () Int)
(declare-fun c2 () Int)
(assert (not (str.contains c0 (str.substr c0 c1 (- c2 c1)))))
(assert (and (<= 0 c1) (<= c1 c2) (<= c2 (str.len c0))))
(check-sat)

(get-model)
```

Executing Z3 ef7915858bca5e759ee0a34df2ae976e7e507bef with the command

    z3 SMTLIB2_COMPLIANT\=true -t\:12000
on [this SMT script](http://www.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/~heizmann/20150912-Z3InterpolationProblem-avg.c.smt2.zip), I obtain the following error message.

    (error "line 74616 column 3676: Sort mismatch at argument #2 for function (declare-fun * (Real Real) Real) supplied sort is Int")

However, there is no *Real* in the script at all.

issue_title: Export of Extension: File not written problem
issue_body:
I'm trying to run the the below code from the MeiterLab example notebook to create an extension. For some reason, only the namespace.yaml but not the extension.yaml gets written on export so that the load of the namespace fails due to missing file. 

```
from pynwb.spec import NWBNamespaceBuilder, NWBGroupSpec, NWBAttributeSpec, NWBDatasetSpec
from pynwb import get_class, load_namespaces
import os

# Define where our extensions should be stored
output_dir = "'/Volumes/Data Drive/nwb_convert_tutorial"
ext_output_dir  = os.path.join(output_dir, "extensions")
ns_name = 'crcnsret1'
ns_path  = os.path.join(ext_output_dir, '%s.namespace.yaml' % ns_name)
ext_path = '%s.extensions.yaml' % ns_name

# Check to make sure that our storage location for the extensions exists
if not os.path.exists(ext_output_dir):
    os.makedirs(ext_output_dir)

# Build the namespace
ns_builder = NWBNamespaceBuilder('Extension for use in my Lab', ns_name)

# Create a custom ImageSeries to add our custom attributes and add our extenions to the namespace
mis_ext = NWBGroupSpec('A custom ImageSeries to add MeisterLab custom metadata',
                       attributes=[NWBAttributeSpec('x' , 'int', 'meister x', required=False),
                                   NWBAttributeSpec('y' , 'int', 'meister y', required=False),
                                   NWBAttributeSpec('dx', 'int', 'meister dx', required=False),
                                   NWBAttributeSpec('dy', 'int', 'meister dy', required=False),
                                   NWBAttributeSpec('pixel_size', 'float', 'meister pixel size', required=False)],
                       neurodata_type_inc='ImageSeries',
                       neurodata_type_def='MeisterImageSeries') 
ns_builder.add_spec(ext_path, mis_ext)

# Export the YAML files for our namespace and extensions
ns_builder.export(ns_path)

# Load the namespace to register it with PyNWB
load_namespaces(ns_path)
```

I tried to just copy the YAML files from previous runs into the folder but loading those did not work (maybe because the format has changed)

**NOTE** You will need to change output_dir before running the example. 
**TEST** It might be worth making a unit/integration test out of this small example. We can just use pythons tempfile library to create and clean-up temporary files.

issue_title: Get return value from function call (call_state)
issue_body:
If using `proj.factory.call_state;  path = proj.factory.path(state);  path1 = path.step(num_inst=1)` to symbolically execute a function, how to get the return value (r0 in ARM) when the function returns?

I tried to use deadend to find it, but there is no deadend for `pg = proj.factory.path_group(path)` found.

I also tried to directly return the `r0` value when the execution finishes, but it only returns one value and usually an "over-executed" one.

I also tried to find return-like instructions at the end or middle of the function, regarding it as a hooking point. But there is no such hook, and usually there are tail calls or something else at the end of a function.

issue_title: Issue may cause headache, networkx.DiGraph() and add_edge
issue_body:
Hello Guys, 

I am reporting something I stumbled upon today and before starting with the Issue: This may not concern you at all, but from my experience with angr's  CFGFast / .. from over a year ago it seemed it may be an issue. 

The problem lies within how networkx.DiGraph() handles the add_edge(node_1, node_2) method and I am trying to describe the problem as simple as possible. I first encountered the problem when loading a serialized networkx.DiGraph() from a database, the core problem is the following code:

```
sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(CFGNode(fromaddr, fromsize, sit_cfg_abstraction),
                                                            CFGNode(toaddr, tosize, sit_cfg_abstraction))
```

The problem already occurs with a linear chain of blocks, lets say 3 blocks with the relationship (succ, pred) leading to a code like this:
```
n1 <--> n2 <--> n3
sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(CFGNode(0x00, 4, sit_cfg_abstraction),
                                                            CFGNode(0x4, 4, sit_cfg_abstraction))

sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(CFGNode(0x04, 4, sit_cfg_abstraction),
                                                            CFGNode(0x8, 4, sit_cfg_abstraction))

```

Despite creating the CFGNode for addr 0x4 two times, leading to different id(..)'s, the creating networkx.DiGraph holds only 3 nodes, because existing nodes (by __hash__) won't be added to DiGraph.nodes. So far, this is what it's supposed to be. 

However, for further simplification, above code can be written as:

```
n1 <--> n2 <--> n3
sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(n1, n2)
sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(x2, n3)

with __hash__(n2) == __hash__(x2)
```

with x2 never ending in the graph.nodes dict. However, x2 makes its way to the predecessor relationships of the graph, without being in the nodes dictionary. Let's take a look into the networkx code:

```
add_edge(self, u, v, ..):
       ...
        if u not in self.succ:
            self.succ[u]= self.adjlist_dict_factory()
            self.pred[u]= self.adjlist_dict_factory()
            self.node[u] = {}
        if v not in self.succ:
            self.succ[v]= self.adjlist_dict_factory()
            self.pred[v]= self.adjlist_dict_factory()
            self.node[v] = {}
        # add the edge
        datadict=self.adj[u].get(v,self.edge_attr_dict_factory())
        datadict.update(attr_dict)
        self.succ[u][v]=datadict
        self.pred[v][u]=datadict
```

The first iteration leads to:

```
sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(n1, n2)
leading to:
node = {n1, n2}
succ = {n1 -> n2, n2 -> {} }
pred = {n1 -> {}, n2 -> n1}
```

But the problem comes with the second iteration
```
sit_cfg_abstraction.function_cfg_dict[graphstartnodeaddr].add_edge(x2, n3)
leading to:
node = {n1, n2, n3}  #(if x2 not in self.succ: is false, since hash(x2) = hash(n2) and n2 is in the list) 
succ = {n1 -> n2, n2 -> n3, n3 -> {}} #self.succ[x2][n3] = .. works, since hash(x2) == hash(n2) 
pred = {n1 -> {}, n2 -> n1, n3 -> x2} #self.pred[n3][x2] = .. problem
```

And here we finally arrived at the problem. Whenever I want to get graph.predecessors(n3), i will receive x2 which is not in the graph in the first place. More problematic, altering the nodes in the graph has no effect for predecessor nodes. And by all means, there are duplicate nodes which consume memory. 

So, I can fix this on application side - and maybe you guys did it in some way, didn't check it thoroughly - but maybe this is also an unnoticed Issue for you. In my opinion, this is a general networkx problem and should be addressed there with something like:

```
if u not in self.succ:
            self.succ[u]= self.adjlist_dict_factory()
            self.pred[u]= self.adjlist_dict_factory()
            self.node[u] = {}
else:
            u = self.node[u]
```

Tell me when you stopped reading ;)

issue_title: Fix OLCI and other readers' file patterns to work on Windows

issue_body:
#### Problem description

This was brought up in the changes in my most recent PR (#123). Right now to compare PROJ.4 strings we sort the parameters by keys then compare the result. This is good enough since PROJ.4 has a lot of defaults and different ways that a ellipsoid can be defined. We could try using something like https://github.com/jswhit/pyproj/issues/15#issuecomment-208862786, but it would require gdal in pyresample which isn't great.

issue_title: Fuzzy matching bug with Flash files
issue_body:
This happens with pywb version 2.0.3 installed via pip on Python 3.6.5:

Pywb returns wrong data for certain URL requests, perhaps related to `.swf` (Flash) files.

To reproduce:

## Create WARC file

Directory structure:
```
files
├── path1
│   └── stuff
│       └── deep-down.html
└── path2
    ├── deep-down
    │   ├── deep-down.html
    │   ├── image.png
    │   └── what.swf
    └── index.html
```

The files are all having arbitrary contents.

Create a WARC file from this:

```bash
$ warcit -o http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/ files -v
[DEBUG] Writing "http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path1/stuff/deep-down.html" (text/html) @ "2018-06-13T08:25:37Z" from "files/path1/stuff/deep-down.html"
[DEBUG] Writing "http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path2/index.html" (text/html) @ "2018-06-13T08:29:18Z" from "files/path2/index.html"
[DEBUG] Adding auto-index: http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path2/ -> http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path2/index.html
[DEBUG] Writing "http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path2/deep-down/image.png" (image/png) @ "2018-06-12T14:05:18Z" from "files/path2/deep-down/image.png"
[DEBUG] Writing "http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path2/deep-down/deep-down.html" (text/html) @ "2018-06-13T08:27:30Z" from "files/path2/deep-down/deep-down.html"
[DEBUG] Writing "http://www.website.co.br/~dinosaurs/t/path2/deep-down/what.swf" (application/x-shockwave-flash) @ "2018-06-13T08:39:31Z" from "files/path2/deep-down/what.swf"
[INFO] Wrote 6 resources to files.warc.gz
```

## Add to pywb

```bash
wb-manager add test files.warc.gz
wayback
```

## Access on localhost:

Requesting a path that partly matches that of any resource in the WARC will result in the flash file being served instead of a not found error.

![selection_999 725](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/571494/41340801-7e31291c-6ef8-11e8-8c9b-e57ac82d76a0.png)

![selection_999 726](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/571494/41340807-83cbb9f0-6ef8-11e8-93c1-d03d0815d871.png)

![selection_999 727](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/571494/41340817-897c2f1a-6ef8-11e8-8e68-d2be30c2bc47.png)

Only when a path is requested that doesn't match parts of resource URLs in the WARC, an error is served:

![selection_999 729](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/571494/41340984-e9a0b76c-6ef8-11e8-863d-e1e4008bbdab.png)

## Data

[WARC file for testing](https://github.com/webrecorder/pywb/files/2097408/files.warc.gz)

This is the isolation of many issues encountered during several projects created with warcit.

issue_title: Mutation Error: "mutate() takes 4 positional arguments but 5 were given"

issue_body:
There are a handful of issues in the current implementation:

- Duplication between `LinuxWorkersManager` and `WindowsWorkersManager`. `worker_cadence` can probably be refactored to be agnostic to the actual worker, so it is just a loop that executes the install function and passes it the configuration.
- Confusingly named variables (execution_scripts is actually a dictionary of configuration data for workers and their parameters).
- Variables to the classes are actually parameters for the worker that is being called. Particularly `s3` and `salt_states`. This should be un-coupled.
- `system_params` and `execution_scripts` are dictionaries with largely undefined structures. Should probably make these objects or otherwise better define the expected structure.

issue_title: Ignore outliers affects scaling but not smoothing
issue_body:
The "ignore outliers" is feature is a great idea. Most training curves start from a nonsensical first point that is best ignored. However, it should also apply to smoothing. In its present implementation, you get correct scale but a "smoothed" curve that still starts at the nonsensical first point and slowly descends towards real data, even if in reality, it had only one initial nonsensical point and descended towards normal values immediately.

An easier heuristic-free implementation would be to just ignore all data points before N. For scaling, smoothing, everything. Also, the first few points are always nonsensical but I usually do care about outliers that happen during training.

issue_body:
I am trying to implement Bilinear CNN [http://vis-www.cs.umass.edu/bcnn/docs/bcnn_iccv15.pdf](url) using tflearn.

[https://drive.google.com/file/d/0B-xUE7zSpUYfcUkxNE1UUVZZU0E/view?usp=sharing](url)


The code mentioned above implements Bilinear CNN as described in the paper. It takes 2 Vgg16 pretrained networks. All FC layers are discarded. Outputs of the conv5_3 layers are used as a feature extractor. Inputs are resized to 3x448x448 instead of the original 3x224x224. Hence the output size of the con5_3 layers will be ==> [batch_size] x 28 x 28 x 512. This is resized to get ==> [batch_size] x 784 x 512. So consider 2 VGG16 layers A and B. Each has output size of ==> [batch_size] x 784 x 512. These networks A and B are then combined using a matrix multiplication, tf.batch_matmul(A.T,B) which gives the output of size ==> [batch_size] x 512 x 512. This output is flattened to give output size of ==> [batch_size] x 262144. On this 1-D tensor, signed square root is applied followed by the l2_normalization. At the end a fully connected layer is added with size ==> 262144 x 100[number of classes]. 

I am unable to train this model.
How can i use tf operations in tflearn.

issue_title: Training an RNN to generate a sine wave
issue_body:
I'm attempting to do this but the predicted values (in green) don't look anything like a sine wave.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1016303/15698647/fac9e1ae-2809-11e6-8904-f96fa0a27d68.png)

I've tried various learning rates, optimizers, number of units, length of history for training, number of steps I try to look ahead. I'm not yet seeing what the problem is, so I would be grateful for suggestions.

```
# Simple example using recurrent neural network to predict time series values

from __future__ import division, print_function, absolute_import

import tflearn
from tflearn.layers.normalization import batch_normalization
import numpy as np
import math
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

step_radians = 0.01
steps_of_history = 100
steps_in_future = 2
index = 0

x = np.arange(0, 4*math.pi, step_radians)
y = np.sin(x)

# Put the data into the right shape
while (index+steps_of_history+steps_in_future < len(y)):
	window = y[index:index+steps_of_history]
	target = y[index+steps_of_history+steps_in_future]
	if index == 0:
		trainX = window
		trainY = target
	else:
		trainX = np.vstack([trainX, window])
		trainY = np.append(trainY, target)
	index = index+1
trainX.shape = (index, steps_of_history, 1)
trainY.shape = (index, 1)

# Network building
net = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, steps_of_history, 1])
net = tflearn.simple_rnn(net, n_units=512, return_seq=False)
net = tflearn.dropout(net, 0.5)
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 1, activation='linear')
net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='sgd', loss='mean_square', learning_rate=0.001)

# Training
model = tflearn.DNN(net, clip_gradients=0.0, tensorboard_verbose=0)
model.fit(trainX, trainY, n_epoch=150, validation_set=0.1, show_metric=True, batch_size=128)

# Prepare the testing data set
# testX = window to use for prediction
# testY = actual value
# predictY = predicted value
index = 0
while (index+steps_of_history+steps_in_future < len(y)):
	window = y[index:index+steps_of_history]
	target = y[index+steps_of_history+steps_in_future]
	if index == 0:
		testX = window
		testY = target
	else:
		testX = np.vstack([testX, window])
		testY = np.append(testY, target)
	index = index+1
testX.shape = (index, steps_of_history, 1)
testY.shape = (index, 1)

# Predict the future values
predictY = model.predict(testX)

# Plot the results
plt.figure(figsize=(20,4))
plt.suptitle('Prediction')
plt.title('History='+str(steps_of_history)+', Future='+str(steps_in_future))
plt.plot(y, 'r-', label='Actual')
plt.plot(predictY, 'gx', label='Predicted')
plt.legend()
plt.savefig('sine.png')

```

issue_title: [tx model] Fall back on version of the server for version
