This issue proposes to remove the `version` field on the transaction model since the version of the transaction model is currently tightly coupled to that of the server, and the server cannot process different schema versions.

Or perhaps, put in the form of a question: "Could we simplify the transaction payload by removing the version for now since it is tightly coupled to the server version, and the server does not currently handle multiple schema versions?"

Although arguably minimally, this could help in simplifying client code that generate payloads, and would also simplify server code.

issue_title: csvsql: When not supplying password, execution stops
issue_body:
When a connection string doesn't contain a password, but the connection needs one, csvsql stops executing (error: `(psycopg2.OperationalError) fe_sendauth: no password supplied`). 

The psql command-line program on the other hand asks for a password. Behaviour can be altered with the --password and --no-password arguments. This option would be valuable (I use both psql and csvsql in the same script)
https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/9151

Right now the help command just gives you an alphabetical columnar list of commands. It would be nice if there was a way to create groups or categories of commands, so you could have related commands show up together.

Instead of this:

```
Documented commands (type help <topic>):
========================================
config     expire       load      resources   shell                stop
connect    findleakers  py        restart     shortcuts            threaddump
deploy     help         pyscript  serverinfo  show                 undeploy
edit       history      quit      sessions    sslconnectorciphers  version
exit       license      redeploy  set         start                vminfo
exit_code  list         reload    settings    status               which
```
It would be nice if you could create something like:
```
Documented commands (for more info type help <command>)

Connecting
=========================================
connect     which

Application Management
=========================================
list
deploy       redeploy      undeploy
start        restart       stop
sessions     expire        findleakers

Server Information
==========================================
resources         serverinfo      sslconnectorciphers
status            thread dump     vminfo

Settings
==========================================
config       set           show

Other
=============================
edit      help        license
exit      history     load
version
```

It would be great if you could not only control the sections of the help, but the order and formatting of the commands within each section. Commands not specified for inclusion in a specific section would be featured in a catch all section like **Other**.

It might also be interesting to have a way to specify a short phrase of help for each command, with some command like **help --long** to show:

```
Documented commands (for more info type help <command>)

Connecting
=========================================
connect    connect to a tomcat server
which      show which server you are currently connected to
...
```

issue_title: Extra parameter for PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_INT to specify arguments `call value policy`
issue_body:
Dear Pybind11 team,

Would it be possible to add extra argument to PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_INT macro for specifying `pybind11::return_value_policy` for function arguments? I have functions in my project that require special custom return_value_policy so i have to expand PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_INT 'by hands'. But PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_INT code became quite volatile so this approach became error prone and require frequent refactoring due to Pybind11 updates.

Thanks,

(@dean0x7d)

One of the changes in PR #321 (18319d5598e874168b00048bf093721a8755c611) invokes pip during cmake to install pytest if it isn't found, but this seems conceptually wrong: a cmake invocation really shouldn't be modifying anything outside the project dir.

I propose that this should be changed to produce a fatal error if pytest isn't found (perhaps with a message to invoke `pip install pytest` or `pip install --user pytest`), or at the very least protected behind a disabled-by-default PYBIND11_INSTALL_DEPS option.  Otherwise we're installing something *outside* of the pybind11 directory without asking just because someone decides to configure the pybind11 test code build.

(As a side note, even with the option approach, I think the debian builds should go back to install `python$PY-pytest` instead of `python$PY-pip python$PY-setuptools`—part of the point of those builds was to install all deps via native system packages rather than via `pip`; it also ensures that everything works as expected without pip/setuptools available.)

issue_body:
Per https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/wildcard-base-domain-two-txts-timeout-problem/56105/3, it's not obvious to users that a later DNS challenge isn't meant to *replace* an earlier DNS challenge. This makes sense because Certbot doesn't explicitly explain at what point the CA is going to validate the challenges; a user could easily assume that the CA validated the old challenge right away and that it's no longer needed. (In fact, Certbot could probably even have been implemented that way!)

So, the text in the manual authenticator should probably explicitly say something like "Don't remove, disable, or replace the earlier challenges yet" when telling the user to set up each new challenge for a subsequent name.

issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-dnsimple` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_dnsimple.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation! 

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase. 

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!). 

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Snapshot of entire state as a WAL recovery optimization
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

When recovering from the Write Ahead Log we would need to replay all the state changes that reside in it. This may turn out to be too many state changes if the Raiden node has been running for weeks/months. 

## Solution

In order to mitigate this we can be taking a snapshot of the state in particular intervals and then during recovery apply the snapshotted state and then replay only the state changes that would be coming on top of it.

## Things needing discussion

When do we take a snapshot? 

- After every single state change  (simplest way, but possibly overkills)
- Every `x` minutes
- After a transfer has been complete.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Take a snapshot of the current state in particular intervals. This means snapshot of both the channel state ( needs https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/507 to work) and the transfer states

According to [IETF](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.2.2), one of the fields in an OAuth2 response should be ``expires_in``.

So far all the providers I've worked with return this field but I have no way to work with it because a time stamp of the access token is not saved. 
I've added a pipeline where I add ``expires_on`` to the EXTRA_DATA that is basically:

     expires_on = int(time.time()) + int(expires_in)

This way I can get my ``access_token`` allways valid (django code!):

    social = request.user.social_auth.get(provider='my-backend')
    if social.extra_data['expires_on'] <= int(time.time()):
        strategy = load_strategy()
        social.refresh_token(strategy)
    return social.extra_data['access_token']

I have availability to do this improvement myself (fork, push) but I would like to have some feedback.

My idea would be to alter the class ``BaseOAuth2`` (OAuth1 doens't have expires_in, at least in RFC) with:

    expires_in = response.get('expires_in', '') or \
                               kwargs.get('expires_in')
    if expires_in not '':
    	data['psa_expires_on'] = int(time.time()) + int(expires_in)
    else:
    	data['psa_expires_on'] = None

I added ``psa`` to the beginning because at least AzureAD Oauth2 protocol replies with ``expires_on``, precisely with this implementation (epoch time)

In the long term, something interesting would be to include a method, like the one I have at the beginning of this issue, to obtain the access token. It would be an alternative to ``social.extra_data['access_token']`` and would assure the token obtained is valid.

issue_title: social_core setup.py may detect wrong python version when using pipenv
issue_body:
With the new pipenv, which is [recommended by python.org now](https://packaging.python.org/new-tutorials/installing-and-using-packages/#installing-pipenv), there are issues with using os.sys_version in setup.py to determine which dependencies to install.

It appears that since pipenv technically runs in your main python environment rather than inside the virtualenv it manages, setup.py detects that python version.  So for example I use python 3.6 as my main python, but have a few legacy projects which are on 2.7 for now.  If I use pipenv then social_core's setup.py sees python 3.6 in os.sys_version and pops python3-openid into the requirements rather than python-openid.

Would it be possible to use the `extras_require` feature of setuptools 18.0 and up to handle the conditional dependency?

Please also see https://github.com/kennethreitz/pipenv/issues/924

issue_title: Need to package pyarrow along with our wheels.
issue_body:
In #692, we started building building pyarrow (that is, if you run Ray's `setup.py`, then inside of the `setup.py` script, we call pyarrow's `setup.py`).

There are a couple of problems here.

1. Pyarrow isn't included in the wheels that we build, so they have a missing dependency and won't work (we don't want to just `pip install pyarrow` because we want to compile specific versions of pyarrow). Though ideally later when things are more stable we'll just `pip install pyarrow`.
2. The current approach may cause problems for people who are using pyarrow separately from Ray. Perhaps the version of pyarrow that we're using should only be installed locally to Ray, that is, you could import it with `import ray.pyarrow`.

It would be great if running task could somehow find some information about itself. For now, at least, its ObjectID. So when I run `fun.remote()` I get ObjectID to the caller. But callee does not have it. It would be great to have it so that we could use this ID for some additional communication between task and caller (debugging and stuff).

issue_title: Speeding up serialization of pandas dataframes.
issue_body:
See earlier related discussion at dask/distributed#614, some related discussion in apache/arrow#1360.

If a dataframe consists of numerical data, it should already be handled efficiently. However, if it contains strings, then those columns are represented in pandas as numpy object arrays. We currently serialize numpy object arrays by converting them to lists and then serializing the lists. While iterating through the list, if we encounter an object that pyarrow doesn't know how to serialize, then we call a custom serializer.

This can often be made faster by just pickling the numpy object array (which is what the pyarrow `pandas_serialization_context` does). However, that fails to handle objects in the numpy array which require custom serializers (custom user-defined objects) or objects that can't be pickled.

In particular, we'd like to support serializing numpy arrays of `ObjectID`s, but `ObjectID`s are not pickleable (see #1453).

**Some options:**
- Make ObjectIDs pickleable (this is simple, but could make it easier to write incorrect code).
- Catch the exception when pickle fails on the numpy object array and then back off to the list approach (this will work in the `ObjectID` case because we raise an exception during pickling, but usually the exception occurs when unpickling, and that will be much harder to handle).
- Store some extra information that lets the serialization context know that it is dealing with a numpy object array that came from a pandas dataframe. This could be done by wrapping the numpy object array in a custom class which is only used internally in Ray or pyarrow.

cc @devin-petersohn @pcmoritz

issue_title: Failure of an actor constructor does not prevent subsequent methods from working.
Add a way to pass context data to overridden template
issue_body: Hello, Currently there is no way to send context data to a overridden template. For instance if you wanted to add a icon to a button. It would be nice to do something like this in your helper: ``` python Button( 'Save', css_class='btn-primary', template='crispy-forms/icon-button.html', template_context={'icon': 'flopy-disk'} ) ``` where icon-button.html looks like: ``` html ``` I don't think adding support for this would be too difficult, adding it to BaseInput could look like this (the only lines I've added have "template_context" on them): ``` python class BaseInput(TemplateNameMixin): """ A base class to reduce the amount of code in the Input classes. """ template = "%s/layout/baseinput.html" def __init__(self, name, value, **kwargs): self.name = name self.value = value self.id = kwargs.pop('css_id', '') self.attrs = {} if 'css_class' in kwargs: self.field_classes += ' %s' % kwargs.pop('css_class') if 'template_context' in kwargs: self. template_context = kwargs.pop('template_context') self.template = kwargs.pop('template', self.template) self.flat_attrs = flatatt(kwargs) def render(self, form, form_style, context, template_pack=TEMPLATE_PACK, **kwargs): """ Renders an `` if container is used as a Layout object. Input button value can be a variable in context. """ self.value = Template(text_type(self.value)).render(context) template = self.get_template_name(template_pack) context.update({'input': self}) if hasattr(self, 'template_context'): context.update(self.template_context) return render_to_string(template, context. ``` If you think this is a good idea I can add it to the other layout objects and put in a pull request.

Hi everyone!

Since the inception of pdfminer.six, a lot of improvements have been made, and several issues have been fixed. I would first like to thank every contributor for having kept this project alive! We are all well aware of the difficulties of parsing PDF documents, and I am sure pdfminer.six has made it easier  for developers to extract information from PDFs. 

But, there are more issues cropping up, and a lot of PRs are pending as well. Documentation, too, is pending. With the increase in these incomplete tasks, it is time that we decide on how to take this project forward. There needs to be a road map created for the future development of this project. For starters, I have reached out to an audience through the dev.to platform - [dev.to](https://dev.to/ganeshtata/comment/3mbc)  so that more people can become aware of this wonderful project, and start contributing.

I myself am new to open source, and I have been the admin of this project for sometime. Sadly, I haven't been able to give it much time, but I am sure that if we can make a good plan for the future of this project, the quality of the project can be improved. I would LOVE to hear your thoughts on this!

issue_title: Some sketch of how to use settings to provide an API object to the handlers
issue_body:
Let's say you have a Python `Git` class that has methods such as `status`, `add`, etc. that talk to git using the command line. You want to use that class in all your handlers.

In your server extension code you will instantiate one of these `Git` objects:

```python
def load_jupyter_server_extension(nbapp):
    git = Git() # The Python class all of your handlers will need/use
    nbapp.web_app.settings['git'] = git
    setup_handlers(nbapp.web_app)
```

Then to access that from your handlers, you will want a property on all your handlers (in a common base class:

```python
@property
def git(self):
    return self.settings['git']
```

Then in your actual handler methods you can use this instance, like this:

```python

class StatusHandler(BaseHandler):

    def get(self):
        result = self.git.status(..)

```

**Current behaviour:**

When joining a command with timeout and timeout was reached, the call returns without any exception making it extremely hard to know whether the command finished successfully or has timedout.

**Desired behaviour:**
`pssh.pssh2_client.ParallelSSHClient.join()`
should either raise an exception when timeout was reached or use another method to notify the caller that the command did not complete successfully

issue_title: Parse out additional open graph object types
issue_body:
See http://ogp.me/#types.

The following

```html
<meta property="og:type" content="article" />
<meta property="article:author" content="https://www.facebook.com/bbcnews" />
<meta property="article:section" content="England" />
```

Should yield something like the following

```json
{
    "article": {
        "author": "https://www.facebook.com/bbcnews",
        "section": "England"
    }
}
```

In addition, if `article:published_time` is found it should populate `publish_date`.

issue_title: Centralise documentation for the output widget
issue_body:
Currently, documentation for the output widget is scattered across:
 - [async widgets](https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/examples/Widget%20Asynchronous.html#Interacting-with-output-widgets-from-background-threads)
 - [widget list](https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/examples/Widget%20List.html#Output)

With PR #1934 , we need to extend the docs for the output widget to expose its use as a decorator. 

Given the output widget's power, we think it makes sense to bring docs on the output widget to the top level. I think it would be useful to structure the notebook as:

 - introduction (what's currently in the widget list)
 - displaying rich output (e.g., with an example from matplotlib?)
 - async widgets
 - using the `@capture` decorator, including as a way to debug widget callbacks.

Happy to tackle this today / this weekend, unless someone else is particularly keen to.

---

issue_title: Is it possible to save flagfiles?
issue_body:
I asked this question [on SO](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50202755/whats-the-idiomatic-way-of-performing-conversions-from-python-gflags-to-yaml-an) but it seems there are not enough people using this flag library there to answer it (or there may be no good answer).

Given an invocation of some python application using gflags, which may or may not rely on flag files, e.g., `python myapp.py --flagfile someflagfile.txt --some_other_flag x`, it is very useful to be able to recover the structured configuration of the invocation (for instance, I'm performing experiments and would like to record the configuration used to perform the experiment in JSON along with the experiment logs).

My current solution is the following manual one, but I was hoping there was a better way of doing it

```
from absl import flags
d = flags.FLAGS.flags_by_module_dict()
d = {k: {v.name: v.value for v in vs} for k, vs in d.items()}
```

The only issue with the above is that if I say, print out the dict `d` as JSON, I can't feed it back in as a `--flagfile` again.

issue_title: Early detection model A extends model A
issue_body:
When building a extended model, the plan is that you've defined:
model A
model B extends model A.

It happens that people building the model are not very awake and they formulate model B extends model B.

Right now the result after some parsing time is: 'RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object'.

It would be nice if pymoca detects this 'model A extends model A' issue early and that the erorr message is something like: Error: in current formulation 'model A extends model A'

issue_title: casadi load_model() api replaces parameter values in expressions
issue_body: When loading variable metadata from a cached model, the ```load_model()``` method does three calls of the ```variable_metadata_function()```. In the process, symbolic expressions that contain parameters with known values are resolved into a numeric value prematurely, so that parameter values cannot be overwritten later. Effectively, cached models behave as though the ```replace_parameter_values``` compiler option is true.

issue_title: 在其他手机助手运行的同时运行adb指令可能导致的编码异常问题
issue_body:
当pc上运行了其他手机助手时，可能会因为端口占用的问题，导致没法运行自己的adb指令：

```
List of devices attached
adb server version (31) doesn't match this client (39); killing...
error: could not install *smartsocket* listener: cannot bind to 127.0.0.1:5037:
通常每个套接字地址(协议/网络地址/端口)只允许使用一次。 (10048)

This application has requested the Runtime to terminate it in an unusual way.
Please contact the application's support team for more information.
could not read ok from ADB Server
* failed to start daemon *
error: cannot connect to daemon

里面的中文报错信息编码被adb处理成了utf-8编码，在airtest中运行adb指令时因为decode的编码是gbk，导致抛出异常：

UnicodeDecodeError: 'gbk' codec can't decode bytes in position 106-107: illegal
multibyte sequence
```

issue_title: Unable to install directly from repo
issue_body:
When trying to install `conu` directly from the this repo—using any of the below mentioned methods—the command fails throwing an error like the following —

_Installation methods_

- `$ pip3 install --user .`
- `$ python3 setup.py install`
- `$ pip3 install git+git://github.com/user-cont/conu.git`

_Error message_
```console
[sumit@hal9000 conu]$ pip3 install --user .
Processing /home/sumit/Coding/conu
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/tmp/pip-lf6poj06-build/setup.py", line 75, in <module>
        install_requires=get_requirements()
      File "/tmp/pip-lf6poj06-build/setup.py", line 35, in get_requirements
        with open("./requirements.txt") as fd:
    FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: './requirements.txt'
...
```

It's clear that it's happening because `setup.py` still contains a reference to a `requirements.txt`, but it was renamed to `requirements.in`. I'm not entirely sure why it was done, I know it was done because of the `Kebechet` integration, but doesn't `Kebechet` also support the `.txt` file extension for the _requirements_ file?

Fixing it would be simple anyway, just changing the reference in `setup.py` to that of `requirements.in`. But I just wanted to ask if we should revert to using the `txt` extension, it's pretty much the standard.

Either way, I can start a pull-request. Just let me know which path should we take. Excited to start contributing to this project :)

issue_title: Grammar compilation has combinatorial performance for ranges and optionals
issue_body:
This is due to the naively aggressive inlining policy of the grammar compiler.

For example, the following grammar takes a long time to compile:
```python
start: a? b? c? d? e? f? g? h? i? j? k? l?

a: "A"
b: "B"
c: "C"
d: "D"
e: "E"
f: "F"
g: "G"
h: "H"
i: "I"
j: "J"
k: "K"
l: "L"
```

Similar issue with exists with ranges, such as `(a~1..10)~1..10`

Obviously, they can also interact, for example `(a? b? c? d?)~1..100`

The solution is a smarter policy, which can extract such expressions into separate rules, when the combinatorical effect passes a certain threshold.

See also: Issue #175

issue_title: GeoIPRegion.dat queries don't work for outside US and Canada
issue_body:
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Grab GeoIPRegion.dat from Maxgrind
2. country1 = country_code_by_name('france2.fr')
3. country2 = country_code_by_addr('212.23.176.132')

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
country1 and country2 should both be 'FR'. It returns empty.

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
Using pygeoip 0.2.2, reproduced on windows and linux platforms.


Please provide any additional information below.
This is working if I'm using the official python package from maxgrind. Another IP address in India (122.174.46.61) is also never resolved. Working with GeoLiteCity.
IP from Canada or US seem to be resolved correctly.

Delete comment Comment 1 by nahor.j, Nov 4, 2011
I believe the issue is in _get_region:
  if i in const.COUNTRY_CODES

I assume that this code is supposed to verify that i is inside the array boundary limits. But what it really does is test if i is a value in the array, which is always false.


And while I'm at it, there is this code in a couple of places in the same function:
  (seek_region // 1000) // 26
which I think should be:
  (seek_region - 1000) // 26
